# Priya's College Strategy
*Naperville, IL | 10th Grade (rising 11th) | Scaffold July 2026*

## Executive Summary

**Projected applicant profile (Fall 2027, when she applies):** Priya projects to a 4.0 unweighted GPA with 10-12 APs at a school offering 25+, which reads as "most demanding curriculum" to any admissions officer. Her 1440 PSAT 10 as a sophomore projects to an SAT in the 1520-1560 range with normal growth and prep. Her spike is unusually clean for a 15-year-old: biomedical research (Science Olympiad state medal in Anatomy & Physiology, cold-emailing professors, Fermilab outreach) plus clinical exposure (Edward Hospital) plus science communication (the newspaper column). Key gap: she has breadth and curiosity but not yet a signature research experience or leadership role, and she says yes to too much. The next 18 months are about depth, not addition.

One thing to say plainly up front: at $310K income with $400K home equity and a brokerage account, you will qualify for essentially zero need-based aid anywhere. The verified net prices below for your income bracket ($110K+) are averages that include families earning $120K. Your actual number at the need-only schools will be at or near sticker. I've shown both.

| # | School | Tier | Round | Est. Net Annual Cost | Financial Risk |
|---|--------|------|-------|---------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Johns Hopkins* | Reach | ED (Nov 1) | $51K-76K, plan high end | Very High |
| 2 | Harvard* | Reach | RD | $62K-78K, plan high end | Very High |
| 3 | Stanford* | Reach | RD | $62K-81K, plan high end | Very High |
| 4 | Duke* | Reach | RD | $63K-82K, plan high end | Very High |
| 5 | Northwestern* | Reach | RD | $59K-81K, plan high end | Very High |
| 6 | WashU* | Reach | RD (ED2 backup) | $53K-74K, plan high end | Very High |
| 7 | USC | Reach | EA (Dec 1) | $59K-80K | High |
| 8 | Case Western | Target | EA (Nov 1) | $48K-79K | Moderate |
| 9 | UIUC | Safety | EA (Nov 1) | $27K-29K | Low |
| 10 | Purdue | Safety | EA (Nov 1) | $27K-31K | Very Low |
| 11 | Indiana Bloomington | Safety | EA (Nov 1) | $26K-33K | Very Low |

*Schools marked with asterisk were on your radar.

**Top 3 things to do in the next 6 months:**
1. **Lock the junior-year course sequence this month** (AP Chem, AP Lang, Calc BC or precalc-to-BC path, APUSH) and consciously decide what she drops. JV tennis is the obvious candidate. Depth beats breadth from here on.
2. **Use this summer (right now) to convert professor emails into a real research placement** for the school year or Summer 2027: UIC, Northwestern Feinberg, UChicago, and Loyola labs are all within reach of Naperville. Simultaneously, start structured SAT prep with a target first sitting of March 2027.
3. **Treat the October 2026 PSAT/NMSQT as a real event.** Her 1440 PSAT 10 puts National Merit Semifinalist genuinely in range (Illinois cutoff has hovered around a 221 Selection Index). NMF status unlocks $28,000+/year at USC and full-tuition-level awards elsewhere. This is the single highest-leverage test she'll take before the SAT.

**The financial floor:** Indiana University Bloomington. Admission is near-certain for her profile (roughly 95%), and the verified average net price for your bracket is about $26,000/year, with even a near-sticker worst case around $33K after typical merit, far below your $60K budget. Purdue and UIUC sit right beside it, all three with sticker prices under your budget before any aid. The worst-case scenario in this plan is a top-40 research university at half your budget. That is a good worst case.

## The Four Threads

Modern admissions at the schools on your radar rewards a coherent story, not a checklist. Priya's story practically writes itself, which is rare. The job is to protect and deepen it, not decorate it.

**Thread 1: The physician-scientist in training.** She's talking about MD/PhD programs at 15 years old, and her behavior backs it up: reading the papers her bio teacher sends, emailing authors, medaling in Anatomy & Physiology. The goal by application time is one substantive research experience with a named mentor and something to show for it (a poster, a paper acknowledgment, a science fair entry, or simply a recommender who can say "she functioned like a grad student"). This is the spine of every essay and the anchor for one recommendation letter.

**Thread 2: The science communicator.** The newspaper science column is a quiet gem. Most research-focused applicants can't write; she can. Grow the column deliberately: interview local researchers, cover the Edward Hospital beat, aim for a real readership artifact by senior fall. This thread differentiates her from the thousands of "research kid" applications JHU and WashU read every year.

**Thread 3: Clinical grounding.** Four hours a week at Edward Hospital is exactly right. Don't add hours; add responsibility. By senior year the goal is a role with patient-facing texture she can write about concretely, and a supervisor who knows her name.

**Thread 4: Twelve years of Indian classical dance.** Do not let her drop this to make room for another club. A 12-year commitment is the rarest thing in an application file, and it's the counterweight that makes the intense science kid a full human being. If a senior recital, arangetram, or teaching-younger-students role emerges naturally, wonderful. If not, longevity alone carries it.

**What not to do:** Don't add new activities. Don't do a pay-to-play "leadership summit." Don't let her run for offices in clubs she doesn't care about. The one thing to consider cutting is JV tennis, which contributes nothing to the narrative and costs 10+ hours a week she doesn't have. You said you worry about burnout more than grades. So do I. The plan should feel like subtraction.

## School List

A note on strategy before the list. Your radar is six schools with admit rates between 3.6% and 12%. That's fine, you were clear-eyed about it, and your budget can absorb them. But it means the early-application decision is the most important strategic choice of senior fall. My default recommendation is **ED at Johns Hopkins**, because it's the best pure fit for her spike, the ED admit rate (11.7%) nearly doubles the overall rate (6.4%), and your family is functionally full-pay everywhere, so ED's main cost (losing the ability to compare aid offers) costs you almost nothing. The alternative is REA at Harvard or Stanford, but note the constraint: REA at either would force USC and Case Western to Regular Decision, which kills USC's December 1 merit scholarship deadline. That's a real price. Revisit this decision after junior-year visits; it's flagged as Decision Point #1.

### 1. Johns Hopkins University (Reach), ED, Nov 1

The best-fit school on your list, full stop. Undergraduate research access at Hopkins is not a brochure claim; it's the institutional culture, and the biomedical ecosystem (the med school, the hospital, public health) is unmatched for a kid with MD/PhD ambitions. Overall admit rate is 6.4%; ED admits at 11.7%. For Priya applying ED with a 1540+, top rigor, and a genuine research narrative, I estimate roughly 12%. Sticker is $88,172. Verified net price for your bracket is $38,745, but that average is dragged down by families near the bottom of the bracket; at your income and assets, plan on paying at or near sticker. Merit exists (5.3% of students, averaging $22,347) but do not build the plan around it. CSS Profile is required for aid applicants, which you won't be. Demonstrated interest is not a meaningful factor, but a summer visit and a specific "why Hopkins" grounded in actual labs and programs matters enormously in the essays. Action: visit Baltimore junior year, and have her identify two or three specific research groups whose work she can speak about credibly.

### 2. Harvard University (Reach), RD

You named it, so here's the honest read. Admit rate is 3.6%. Priya unhooked (no legacy from either of you, not a recruited athlete) is realistically at about 4% even with a perfect file. Harvard now requires test scores, which is fine; hers will be strong. Sticker is $86,926; verified bracket net price is $53,571, but again, at $310K expect sticker or close to it. No merit aid exists, period. Harvard's early round is Single-Choice Early Action, which we're not using because ED at Hopkins is the better strategic play; Harvard therefore goes RD. Demonstrated interest is not tracked. Action: nothing Harvard-specific before senior year. A 4% lottery ticket doesn't reward cultivation; it rewards a great file, which the rest of this plan builds.

### 3. Stanford University (Reach), RD

Same math, same honesty. Admit rate 3.6%, estimate ~4% for Priya. Sticker $90,711; verified bracket net price $50,452; plan on sticker. No merit aid worth planning around (0.6% of students). Stanford's REA restriction is why this is RD under the Hopkins-ED plan. Stanford requires test scores starting with fall 2026 enrollment, so she'll submit. If, after visits, Stanford emerges as the unambiguous first choice, the alternative strategy is Stanford REA, with USC and Case Western pushed to RD and Hopkins to RD or ED2 elsewhere. I'd only make that trade if she falls in love on the ground. Action: fold a campus visit into any California trip junior year.

### 4. Duke University (Reach), RD

Admit rate 5.7% overall; estimate ~6% for Priya in RD. Duke ED admits at 17.3%, and if Hopkins slips in her rankings after visits, Duke ED is a legitimate substitute; that's part of Decision Point #1. Sticker $92,419; verified bracket net price $54,375; plan high. The one genuine lottery worth a free application essay: the **Robertson Scholars Leadership Program**, full cost of attendance. Merit overall reaches 5.3% of students but at a striking $85,600 average, meaning it's a tiny number of huge awards. Duke considers demonstrated interest, unusually for this tier, so she should attend a virtual session and open their emails. Action: apply for Robertson when the time comes; treat it as a bonus, not a plan.

### 5. Northwestern University (Reach), RD

To be precise, this is Northwestern University in Evanston, not Northwestern College in Iowa, which our database also matched when it saw "Northwestern" (very different schools, and I'm confident about which one a Naperville family with this list means). Admit rate 7.7%; estimate ~8% for Priya. The fit case is strong: elite biomedical research 40 minutes from home, and her science-journalism thread resonates at the school that houses Medill. Sticker $92,564; verified bracket net price $48,837; plan on sticker. Merit is effectively nonexistent (0.7% of students). Northwestern offers ED only for early applicants; under the Hopkins plan she applies RD. If Northwestern becomes her clear first choice, ED here (23.0% ED admit rate) is a strong alternative, and the hometown-fit story writes itself. Demonstrated interest is not a significant factor. Action: do the full visit early, precisely because it's close and cheap to do, and it will sharpen the ED decision.

### 6. Washington University in St. Louis (Reach), RD with ED2 backup

Admit rate 12.0%; estimate ~13% for Priya. WashU is arguably the best pre-med environment on this list: collaborative culture, extraordinary advising, and a medical school that welcomes undergrads into labs. It is also the best big-merit lottery for her exact profile. The verified named awards, all full or half tuition: **Danforth Scholars Program**, **John B. Ervin Scholars Program**, **Annika Rodriguez Scholars Program**, and **Ampersand Scholarships** in Arts & Sciences (plus **Langsdorf Fellowships** in engineering and **Distinguished Scholars** in Olin if her direction shifts). These are highly competitive, low-single-digit-probability awards, but she's the kind of applicant they exist for. Sticker $85,760; verified bracket net price $42,712; without a named scholarship, plan near sticker. Strategy: RD, with **ED2 in January as the live option** if Hopkins ED denies in December and WashU is her top remaining choice; note that ED2 may complicate some scholarship timelines, so check WashU's scholarship application deadlines in fall 2027. Action: apply to the scholars programs by their fall deadlines regardless of round.

### 7. University of Southern California (Reach), EA, Dec 1

The merit play among the reaches. Admit rate 9.8%; estimate ~10%. What makes USC different: 79.2% of students receive merit aid averaging $20,703, the **Trustee Scholarship** covers full tuition, and National Merit Finalists receive $28,000+ per year. If Priya makes NMF, USC becomes the rare top-tier private that could land in the $50Ks. Sticker $87,887; verified bracket net price $56,347; with typical merit I estimate $59-80K for you. The catch: merit consideration requires the December 1 EA application, which is exactly why we're not spending the early slot on Harvard/Stanford REA. Strong biomedical research and a huge pre-health infrastructure. Action: apply EA by December 1, complete every scholarship supplement, and flag the NMF box if she earns it.

### 8. Case Western Reserve University (Target), EA, Nov 1

The most underrated school on this list for her. Admit rate 37.8%; with her stats above Case's 75th percentile, I estimate ~42%. World-class biomedical research (Cleveland Clinic and University Hospitals are effectively across the street), and undergrads get real lab roles early. Merit is the point here: 30.7% of students receive it, averaging $30,499, and her profile puts her toward the top of their pool, so I'd put her merit odds around 60%. Named full-tuition awards from the verified data: **Andrew and Eleanor Squire Scholarship** and **A.W. Smith Innovation Scholarship**. Sticker $89,514; verified bracket net price $49,117; my estimate for you is $48-79K, right around budget. Case tracks demonstrated interest and it genuinely matters there: visit, interview, open the emails, engage the regional rep. CSS Profile required only for need aid, which you're skipping. Action: visit junior spring; this is the target that most rewards courtship.

### 9. University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign (Safety), EA, Nov 1

Your flagship, and a legitimately excellent one for biology (MCB is a research powerhouse). Overall admit rate is 42.4%, but that number is dragged down by CS and engineering; as an in-state applicant to a life-sciences major with a 4.0 and a 1520+, her realistic odds are more like 55%. In-state sticker is $33,232 all-in, and the verified net price for your bracket is $28,358. Under budget at sticker, before any aid. Merit exists (12% of students receive awards) but isn't needed for affordability. No CSS Profile. Demonstrated interest isn't a factor. The honors layer (Campus Honors Program) is extremely selective, so treat it as a bonus. Action: apply EA on November 1, major choice matters at UIUC so apply MCB, and visit; it's 90 minutes away and she should know what $27-29K buys before she compares it to $88K.

### 10. Purdue University (Safety), EA, Nov 1

You know this campus already; her brother is there. No sibling admissions boost exists, but the familiarity is worth something real: easy visits, honest intel, and a kid who won't be homesick in a vacuum. Admit rate 49.9% overall, and biology is far less contested than the CS program her brother entered; with stats above Purdue's 75th percentile I estimate ~85%. Out-of-state sticker is a remarkable $38,824 all-in thanks to Purdue's long tuition freeze, and the verified bracket net price is $21,130; plan on $27-31K. Under budget at full sticker with zero aid, which is what makes it a true financial safety. The John Martinson Honors College is worth the separate application. Action: apply EA November 1 and apply to honors.

### 11. Indiana University Bloomington (Safety), EA, Nov 1

The financial floor and the guaranteed good outcome. Admit rate 78.2%, and her stats sit far above IU's 75th percentile; I estimate 95%. Verified bracket net price is $24,018 against a $55,875 out-of-state sticker; with typical aid patterns I'd budget $26-33K, and even a pessimistic case stays under budget. IU has serious research infrastructure and, for the true lottery ticket, the **Wells Scholars Program** (full cost of attendance plus study-abroad funding). The Hutton Honors College takes a separate application and is very gettable for her. Action: apply EA November 1, apply to Hutton, and put Wells on the scholarship calendar.

**What not to do with this list:** Don't add more reaches. Seven schools under 13% is already a lot of essays, and an eighth lottery ticket doesn't change the math, it just steals hours from the applications that do. And don't let anyone talk you into a mid-tier private at $65K that's worse than UIUC at $27-29K just because it's private.

## QuestBridge

Not applicable to your family. QuestBridge serves households earning roughly under $65,000, and at $310K you're several multiples past the threshold. You'll see several of these schools labeled "QuestBridge partners" in materials; ignore it, it's not your pathway.

## Financial Aid Strategy

**The honest headline: you are a full-pay family, and the strategy is merit or in-state, not need.** At $310K income plus assets, every need-based formula in the country returns zero or near-zero.

**CSS Profile and your home equity.** The elite privates on this list use the CSS Profile, which, unlike the FAFSA, counts your roughly $400K in home equity and looks harder at brokerage assets. For families near the aid margins this is a nasty surprise. For you it's academic: you're not near the margins, and the equity just confirms it. My recommendation is to skip the CSS Profile entirely (it costs money and buys you nothing), and file the FAFSA once in fall 2027 only if you want access to federal unsubsidized loans as a cash-flow tool or if a merit program requires it on file. Some merit scholarships at some schools require a FAFSA; check each scholarship's fine print senior fall.

**Illinois MAP Grant.** Naming it because it's your state's flagship program: the Monetary Award Program pays up to $8,064/year, but it requires a Student Aid Index of 9,000 or less. Your family will not qualify, and no strategy changes that. Nothing else in the Illinois state-aid toolkit moves the needle for your income.

**Where the discounts actually live for you:**
1. **In-state and cheap out-of-state publics.** UIUC at ~$27-29K, Purdue at ~$27-31K, IU at ~$26-33K. These are the real price breaks, no application tricks required.
2. **Merit at merit-granting privates.** USC (79.2% of students, avg $20,703, Trustee Scholarship, $28K+/yr for NMF) and Case Western (30.7%, avg $30,499, Squire and A.W. Smith full-tuition awards) are the two on this list where merit is probable, not a lottery.
3. **Named full-ride lotteries.** WashU's scholars programs, Duke's Robertson, IU's Wells. Apply to all of them; expect none of them.
4. **National Merit.** The October 2026 PSAT is the gateway. NMF status converts directly to money at USC and would open full-tuition-scale options at schools not currently on this list (Alabama, Fordham) if you ever wanted a bargain wildcard.

**The 529 math.** $180K covers roughly two years at a full-pay private, all four years at UIUC or Purdue with money left over, or four years at a merit-discounted Case/USC with a modest top-up. Frame the April 2028 decision that way: the question won't be "can we pay for Hopkins," it will be "is Hopkins worth $170K more than UIUC over four years, out of current income and savings." You said you'd stretch for the right school but want to understand what you're buying. Section-by-section, that's what this document is for; the short version is you'd be buying research access, cohort, and pipeline into MD/PhD admissions, and for this specific kid that purchase is more defensible than for most.

**Run the Net Price Calculators anyway,** at every school on the list, junior year. Ten minutes each, and they'll confirm the full-pay picture school by school so April 2028 holds no surprises.

## Summer Plan

**Summer 2026 (right now, rising junior).** It's July, so this is a salvage-and-setup summer, and that's fine. Three jobs: (1) Convert her professor-emailing habit into a concrete ask: a school-year or Summer 2027 placement in a lab at UIC, Northwestern, UChicago, or Loyola. The email that works is specific: "I read your 2024 paper on X, I had a question about the methods in Figure 3, and I'm looking for a volunteer bench role." Her bio teacher, the one feeding her papers, may have contacts; use him. (2) Begin SAT prep now, low intensity, 3-4 hours a week, targeting a March 2027 first sitting and a 1550+ ceiling. The PSAT/NMSQT in October 2026 is the near-term milestone. (3) Draft the Summer 2027 program applications, because the good ones have December and January deadlines. Also acceptable this summer: continued hospital volunteering and unapologetic rest. Do not pay for a generic "pre-med summer intensive."

**Summer 2027 (before senior year, the high-stakes one).** Priority order: (1) A selective research program: RSI (application due December 2026, brutally selective, free), Simons Summer Research Program at Stony Brook, or Stanford's SIMR. These are worth applying to precisely because they're selective; admission is itself a credential. (2) If those don't hit, a mentored placement in the lab she cultivated in 2026, full-time for 6-8 weeks, aiming for a poster or a science-fair-eligible project. A local lab with a real mentor beats a $9,000 brand-name camp every single time, and admissions officers know the difference. (3) In parallel: essays drafted by August 1, 2027, and the Common App activities list written. What not to do: an expensive non-selective "research experience" program, a second unrelated activity, or a summer so packed she starts senior year exhausted.

## Probability and Cost Estimates

These are informed estimates based on Priya's projected profile and verified admit-rate data, not outputs of a statistical simulation. Treat them as planning numbers.

**Headline math:** Across the seven reaches, the estimated chance of at least one admit is roughly 40-50%. Adding Case Western (~42%) pushes the chance of at least one admit at or above Case's tier to roughly 70%. The chance she has UIUC, Purdue, or IU in hand by December 2027 is effectively certain, and at least two of them well under budget.

**The decision scenarios for April 2028:**
1. **Hopkins ED lands (Dec 2027, ~12%).** Done by the holidays. Cost: ~$51-76K/year, ~$204-304K total, roughly half from the 529. You committed to this trade when you signed the ED agreement, which is why the visit-and-decide work happens junior year.
2. **Hopkins misses, a reach hits in RD (~35-40% conditional).** Now you're comparing, say, Duke at $92K sticker against Case with merit at ~$48-79K and UIUC at $27-29K. The prestige-versus-price conversation happens with real numbers on the table.
3. **Reaches miss, USC or Case comes through with merit.** A top-tier research university at $48-80K, inside or near budget. This is a genuinely strong outcome, not a consolation.
4. **Everything above the target line misses (~20-25%).** She's choosing among UIUC MCB, Purdue honors, and IU Hutton, at $26-33K, with the 529 covering everything and money left for medical school. Given her MD/PhD trajectory, this scenario has a real argument for being the smartest one anyway.

**Financial floor, restated:** Indiana Bloomington, ~95% admit probability, $26-33K/year. Worst case is a fine flagship at 40-50% of budget with $100K+ of the 529 preserved for med school.

## What If the Profile Lands Lower

**If the SAT stalls around 1440-1480:** The reaches get harder but the plan holds. Hopkins, Harvard, and Stanford require or will require scores; a 1470 is below their 25th percentiles and drops those from ~4-12% to lottery-ticket-minus. The move: go test-optional where allowed and where her score sits below the 25th percentile (WashU, USC, Case currently allow it; verify policies in fall 2027), and lean harder on Case (still ~40%+), UIUC, Purdue, and IU, none of which blink at a 1460. Consider one more test sitting in August 2027. The financial picture doesn't change at all, because the affordable schools were never score-contingent for her.

**If the GPA takes a hit junior year:** A single B+ in AP Chem changes nothing; don't let anyone catastrophize it. A broader slide to a 3.8 unweighted trims the reach odds by a couple of points each and, more importantly, would tell us the load is wrong. The fix is fewer APs senior year and a visible upward trend, not more tutoring on top of an unsustainable schedule.

**If the research placement never materializes:** Her file is still top-decile: state Science Olympiad medal, clinical hours, the column, 12 years of dance, top rigor. The essays shift from "here's my research" to "here's my intellectual pursuit of research," which her professor correspondence and paper-reading habit genuinely support. Case, UIUC, Purdue, and IU outcomes are unaffected.

**The reassuring bottom line:** the downside scenario in this plan is not "scramble." It's Purdue or UIUC or IU, under budget, with honors options, on a campus where she'll get into a lab by sophomore year anyway. Every branch of this tree ends somewhere good.

## What to Do Now

1. **This month:** Finalize junior-year courses (AP Chem, AP Lang, APUSH, the most advanced math available to her) and make the subtraction decision about tennis. She should also send the lab-placement emails while professors are actually reading email.
2. **By September:** SAT prep underway, PSAT/NMSQT registered through Naperville North, and a family calendar entry for the October 2026 test date. This one test carries a potential $28K+/year at USC.
3. **By December:** RSI and Simons applications submitted for Summer 2027; SIMR in January. Selective and free beats expensive and open-enrollment.
4. **Junior fall and spring:** Visit Northwestern (it's 40 minutes away), UIUC, and Purdue (free intel via her brother). Fold in Hopkins, WashU, and Case around breaks. These visits feed Decision Point #1: ED at Hopkins versus an alternative ED/REA, decided by June 2027.
5. **Ongoing:** Protect the kid. She's intense, organized, and 15 years old. The plan works because of who she already is, not because of anything you add. Your job for the next 18 months is mostly to be the person who says "you're allowed to do less."

## Developmental Roadmap

Priya finished 10th grade with a 4.0 unweighted, one AP under her belt (the max her school allowed), and a state Science Olympiad medal. The roadmap below covers the two years that remain. The theme, as promised in the main brief, is subtraction and depth. Naperville North offers 25+ APs, which means "most demanding curriculum" for her means 10-12 APs total, not 18. Ten to twelve at a school like hers reads exactly the same to an admissions officer as fifteen, except the kid with twelve slept.

### 11th Grade (2026-27): The Year That Matters Most

**Courses:**

| Slot | Course | Notes |
|------|--------|-------|
| Science | AP Chemistry | The natural next step for a bio/chem kid. Her recommender pool starts here. |
| Science 2 (if schedule allows) | AP Biology follow-up is done; consider AP Physics 1 OR skip a second science | Do not double up unless she genuinely wants to. One lab science plus research beats two lab sciences plus exhaustion. |
| Math | AP Calculus BC if she's completed precalc; otherwise Honors Precalc with BC senior year | Either path is fine. BC junior year is the stronger signal but not worth summer-school gymnastics. |
| English | AP English Language | Her writing is a genuine asset. This class feeds the essays she'll write in 12 months. |
| History | AP U.S. History | Standard rigor marker. |
| Language | Continue to level 4 of whatever she's taking | Four years of one language matters at the Harvard/Stanford tier. |
| Elective | Something restorative, not another AP | Orchestra, art, study hall. Seriously. |

That's 4-5 APs junior year, putting her at 5-6 cumulative. On track for 10-12 by graduation without a single overloaded semester.

**Extracurriculars, 11th grade:**
- **Science Olympiad:** Aim for a leadership role (event captain, team officer) and a second state medal. This is her competitive credential; feed it.
- **Research placement:** The lab she's cultivating this summer becomes a school-year commitment, even 4-6 hours a week. This is the single most important addition of junior year, and it only fits because tennis leaves.
- **Newspaper science column:** Continue, and grow it deliberately. Interview a local researcher. Cover something at Edward Hospital. By spring, she should have 8-10 published columns and possibly an editor title.
- **Edward Hospital:** Hold at 4 hours a week. Ask her supervisor about a role with more responsibility, not more hours.
- **Indian classical dance:** Continue. Protect it. If teaching younger students or a senior performance emerges naturally, lovely. Do not manufacture it.
- **JV tennis:** This is the subtraction. If she loves it as pure recreation, she can hit with friends on weekends. The 10+ hours a week of practice and matches are the hours the research placement needs.

**Milestones, 11th grade:**
- **October 2026:** PSAT/NMSQT. The National Merit gateway. Illinois's Selection Index cutoff has hovered around 221, and her 1440 PSAT 10 puts that genuinely in range with prep. Treat the two weeks before it like finals week.
- **December 2026:** RSI application due. Simons Summer Research Program application due around then too.
- **January 2027:** SIMR and other selective summer program deadlines.
- **March 2027:** First SAT sitting. Target 1550+.
- **April-May 2027:** AP exams. May-June: second SAT sitting only if needed.
- **Spring 2027:** Campus visits. Northwestern (40 minutes), UIUC (90 minutes), Purdue (brother intel), Case Western (rewards demonstrated interest more than anyone else on the list). Fold Hopkins and WashU into a break.
- **June 2027:** Decision Point #1. ED at Hopkins, or an alternative (Northwestern ED, Duke ED, Stanford REA). Decide as a family with visit impressions and the SAT score in hand.

Covered in the main brief, but restated for the record:

4. **Also:** Rest. She starts the most stressful fall of her life in August 2027. Do not let her arrive at it already tired.

### What Not to Do, All Grades

Completed a summer outreach program (Summer 2025). This is a finished, real item. It stays on the list as evidence of early initiative; nothing needs to be added to it.

Two years of JV tennis through 10th grade. If she stops after this year per the plan, it's listed honestly as a 9th-10th grade activity. Two years of a sport she enjoyed is a perfectly good line. No spin needed.

## Essay Strategy

### The Common App Personal Statement

**The angle I'd push her toward:** the professor emails. A 15-year-old (16 years old by the time she writes it) who reads a journal article, gets stuck on the methods in Figure 3, and emails the author is showing something no activity list can: intellectual behavior when nobody's watching. The essay isn't "I love science." It's a specific story about one paper, one confusion, one email, and what happened next. Curiosity as a verb.

**A strong alternative:** the dance-and-science essay, if she can avoid the cliché version. Twelve years of classical dance is discipline, embodiment, and anatomy learned from the inside. If she can connect what she knows about her own body from dance to what fascinates her about anatomy and physiology (her medal event, not coincidentally), that's an essay only she can write. The cliché version is "dance taught me discipline which helps me in science." Kill that draft on sight.

**A third option:** the burnout essay, but only if it's true and only if she's actually learned the lesson. "I said yes to everything and here's what I chose to stop doing and why" is a mature, memorable essay. It's also risky if it reads as complaint. Her call.

**What the essay should NOT be:**
- A rehash of the activities list. The reader has the list.
- "I want to be a doctor because I love helping people." Every pre-med essay says this. Hers should sound like someone who reads papers for fun, because she does.
- A tour of her achievements. She has a 4.0 and a state medal; the essay's job is to show the person, not repeat the transcript.
- A hardship narrative. She doesn't have one and shouldn't manufacture one. Comfortable kids write great essays about ideas all the time.
- Written in August panic. Drafted by August 1, 2027, per the roadmap.

### Supplemental Essay Strategy by School

| School | Likely Prompts (verify Summer 2027) | Priya's Angle |
|--------|-------------------------------------|----------------|
| Johns Hopkins | Collaboration/community essay | Name two or three specific labs or research groups whose work she can discuss credibly, plus the science-writing angle (Hopkins values communicators). This essay must prove she's done real homework. The ED application lives or dies here. |
| Harvard | Multiple short essays on intellectual life, activities, identity | The paper-reading habit and the dance longevity give her two distinct registers. Don't spend every essay on science. |
| Stanford | Roommate essay, "what matters and why," intellectual vitality | The roommate essay is where the spreadsheets-for-fun personality shines. Let her be funny. Intellectual vitality essay: the professor emails, again, in different clothes than the Common App version. |
| Duke | Why Duke + optional community essays | Research-specific: name programs, name faculty areas. Also apply to Robertson, which has its own essays about leadership and purpose. |
| Northwestern | Why Northwestern | The strongest "why us" on her list because it's true: elite biomedical research 40 minutes from home, and Medill's presence makes her science-journalism thread land harder here than anywhere. Visit first so the essay has ground truth in it. |
| WashU | Why WashU + scholars program essays | Pre-med advising culture and med school lab access. The Danforth/Ervin/Rodriguez/Ampersand applications need their own essays; budget time in October 2027 for them. |
| USC | Why USC + short answers | Complete every scholarship supplement. USC's short answers are quirky; the dance thread and the spreadsheet personality do well here. Dec 1 deadline is non-negotiable for merit. |
| Case Western | Why Case (and possibly interview) | Cleveland Clinic proximity, early lab access, and specifics. Case tracks interest, so this essay plus a visit plus opened emails is a package. Aim it at the Squire and A.W. Smith scholarships too. |
| UIUC | Major-specific short answers | Why MCB specifically. Concrete: the research areas, the honors options. UIUC admits by major, so this short essay does real work. |
| Purdue | Short answers | Straightforward. Mention the honors college application. Her brother's on campus; genuine familiarity reads well. |
| Indiana | Short answers + Hutton/Wells materials | The Wells Scholars application is the writing-intensive piece here; it goes on the senior fall calendar as its own project. |

**Workload math:** Eleven schools plus scholarship supplements is roughly 25-35 distinct pieces of writing. This is why the Common App essay finishes by August 1 and why the school list doesn't grow.

## Recommendation Letter Strategy

She needs two core academic letters plus a counselor letter, with an optional third from a research mentor. Here's the shape:

**Letter 1: The science teacher who feeds her papers.** You already know who this is. A biology teacher who can write "she asks graduate-level questions and then actually reads the papers I send her" is the single most valuable recommender she has, because he can testify to intellectual behavior beyond the syllabus, which is exactly what Hopkins, Harvard, and Stanford letters are supposed to reveal and almost never do. If he teaches her again junior or senior year, even better, but the relationship already justifies the ask.

**Letter 2: A humanities teacher, ideally AP English Language junior year.** A science applicant with a glowing letter from an English teacher is a rarer and stronger file than one with two science letters. Her writing is genuinely excellent; the letter should say so with an example. If APUSH goes better than AP Lang, that teacher works too. The point is a junior-year core-academic teacher outside STEM.

**Letter 3 (optional supplement): The research mentor.** If the lab placement produces a mentor who supervised her for six-plus months, a short letter saying "she functioned like a graduate student" is worth its weight in gold at research universities. Most schools accept one supplemental letter. Only send it if it adds something the teachers can't say. A generic "Priya was a pleasant volunteer" letter is worse than no letter.

**Counselor letter:** Naperville North counselors carry big caseloads. Priya should make the counselor's job easy: a one-page "brag sheet" covering the four threads (research, communication, clinical, dance), the tennis subtraction and why (that's actually a good story for a counselor to tell: a kid who learned to choose), and the family context. Get this to the counselor by early fall of senior year, and have Priya introduce herself junior spring so the letter isn't written from a file.

**When and how to ask:** Late spring of junior year (April-May 2027), in person, specifically: "I'm applying to research-focused universities, Early Decision at Johns Hopkins with a November 1 deadline. Would you be willing to write me a strong letter?" The word "strong" gives a lukewarm teacher a graceful exit, which she wants. Follow the yes with the brag sheet and a genuine thank-you note in November regardless of outcomes.

**What each letter should convey, in one line each:**
- Science teacher: intellectual hunger beyond the classroom, with the paper-reading story.
- English/humanities teacher: a scientist who writes beautifully and thinks across disciplines.
- Research mentor (if applicable): performed real work at a level above her age.
- Counselor: coherence, character, and the maturity of choosing depth over breadth.

## Supplemental Materials: Research Abstract Guidance

Priya isn't an arts-supplement or maker-portfolio applicant, and she's not a recruit (JV tennis, mercifully, resolves that question). The one supplemental material that matters for her is a **research abstract or brief**, if the lab placement produces real work.

**What it is:** A one-page document: project title, mentor and institution, a 150-250 word abstract in standard scientific format (background, question, methods, findings or progress, significance), plus one sentence stating exactly what Priya personally did. That last sentence matters most. Admissions readers at research universities are allergic to inflated claims; "I performed RNA extractions and maintained cell cultures under Dr. [mentor]'s supervision, and contributed to figure preparation" is credible and strong. "I discovered" is a red flag unless it's literally true.

**Where it goes:** Most schools on her list accept additional materials through the applicant portal after submission, and the Common App additional information section can hold an abstract as plain text. Hopkins and WashU in particular will read it. Check each portal's rules in fall 2027.

**What about the science column?** If she has 8-10 strong published columns by senior fall, a link to the two or three best (in Additional Information, not as a formal portfolio) supports Thread 2. Don't submit all of them. Two great clips beat ten okay ones.

**What not to submit:** A poster PDF with her name fourth on the author list and no explanation, a certificate from a summer program, or anything that duplicates what a letter already says. Supplemental materials should add information, not volume.

## Financial Aid Negotiation Guide

First, a level-set specific to your family: **need-based appeals are off the table.** You won't have a need-based package to appeal, and no story about brokerage volatility changes a $310K income in a formula. What you can work with is merit, and the leverage varies enormously by school.

### How to Compare Offers Side by Side (April 2028)

Build one spreadsheet (Priya can build it; she'll enjoy this part). One row per school, these columns:

1. Sticker cost of attendance (their number, including their travel/personal estimates)
2. Merit awards, with renewal conditions (GPA required? Guaranteed all four years?)
3. Net cost per year
4. Four-year total, assuming their published annual increase (typically 3-4% per year; ask if merit awards grow with tuition or stay flat, because a flat $30K award against rising tuition erodes)
5. 529 coverage: years of school your $180K buys at that net price
6. What's left for medical school

Watch for the classic trap: an award letter that buries a loan in the "aid" total. For your family this mostly won't apply, but if any letter lists a federal loan as "aid," strip it out before comparing. Loans are financing, not discounts.

### Which Schools Have Flexibility

| School | Flexibility | Reality |
|--------|-------------|---------|
| Harvard, Stanford, Northwestern, Duke (non-Robertson) | **None** | Need-only or effectively need-only. There is no merit lever to pull and no one to negotiate with. Don't try; it wastes goodwill. |
| Johns Hopkins | **Essentially none for you** | Merit exists (5.3% of students) but it's awarded at admission, not negotiated after. Also note: if she's admitted ED, you signed away your leverage. That's the known price of the ED strategy, and for a full-pay family it costs little. |
| WashU | **Low, via scholars programs only** | The named scholarships (Danforth, Ervin, Rodriguez, Ampersand) are competitive awards with their own processes, not negotiable line items. Apply by their deadlines; don't expect a post-offer adjustment. |
| USC | **Moderate** | Merit is systematic (Trustee, Presidential, NMF awards). USC occasionally reconsiders merit with a compelling competing offer from a peer school. Worth one polite email if she has a stronger offer from a comparable private. |
| Case Western | **Highest on your list** | Case competes for exactly her profile and knows it loses admits to bigger names. A competing merit offer from a peer (USC, a strong WashU package, even a flagship honors deal) presented politely can move a Case offer. This is the school where a well-written appeal email has real expected value. |
| UIUC, Purdue, IU | **Low** | Public merit is largely formulaic. IU and Purdue honors-related awards are what they are. The prices are already the discount. |

### How to Ask (Case and USC, March 2028)

One email, from Priya (not you), to the admissions or merit scholarship office:

- Tone: grateful and genuinely interested, never adversarial. "Negotiate" is the wrong word out loud; the right phrase is "merit reconsideration."
- Content: "Case remains one of my top choices because [two specific, true reasons]. I've received a merit offer of [amount] from [peer school]. Is there any possibility of a merit review? I'd be glad to send the offer letter."
- Attach the competing offer. Schools verify.
- Expectation: a few thousand a year is a realistic win, occasionally more. Over four years even $3K/year is $12,000 for one email. Worth the twenty minutes; not worth building the plan around.

### The Decision Framework

When the offers land, the question you framed in July 2026 is still the right one: not "can we pay for X" but "is X worth $Y more than UIUC over four years, given that medical school is coming." Write the four-year delta and the projected med school cost (call it $300-400K at private med schools by the early 2030s) on the same page and decide with both numbers visible. A full-pay bachelor's plus full-pay MD is an $800K+ decade. The 529 you built gives you real choices; the spreadsheet makes sure you're choosing on purpose.

## Senior Year Application Timeline (Aug 2027 - May 2028)

**Aug 2027**
- Common App opens Aug 1. Personal statement should already be in near-final draft (per the Summer 2027 plan). Finalize it this month.
- Enter activities list and honors into Common App. Request transcripts through Naperville North's process.
- Confirm Decision Point #1 is locked: Hopkins ED (or the alternative chosen in June 2027). Everything below assumes Hopkins ED.
- Final SAT sitting this month only if the March/May scores fell short of 1520.

**Sept 2027**
- Confirm recommenders (asked in spring; re-confirm now) and deliver the brag sheet to the counselor.
- Draft the Hopkins supplement plus all Nov 1 applications: Case EA, UIUC EA, Purdue EA (plus Martinson Honors), IU EA (plus Hutton).
- Start WashU scholars program essays (Danforth, Ervin, Rodriguez, Ampersand) and IU Wells Scholars materials; check every scholarship deadline on each school's site this month, some fall before the admission deadline.
- If she made National Merit Semifinalist (notification comes early fall), complete the Finalist application promptly. List USC as first choice with NMSC when the timing is right; confirm the current mechanics with the counselor.

**Oct 2027**
- Submit UIUC, Purdue, and IU EA applications early in the month. Do not let safeties pile up behind the ED deadline.
- Finalize and submit Hopkins ED and Case EA by late October (target Oct 25, not Nov 1; portals get flaky).
- Send test scores where required. Verify each school's testing policy one more time; policies move.
- Begin USC application and scholarship supplements.

**Nov 2027**
- Nov 1: Hopkins ED, Case EA, UIUC EA, Purdue EA, IU EA all in.
- Submit USC EA well before Dec 1 (the merit gate).
- Draft all RD applications (Harvard, Stanford, Duke, Northwestern, WashU) now, even though you hope not to send them. Writing them in November is calm; writing them December 15-31 is misery.
- Submit scholars program applications (WashU named scholarships, Wells) per their deadlines.

**Dec 2027**
- Dec 1: USC EA deadline. Confirm submission.
- Mid-December: Hopkins ED decision.
  - **Admitted:** Withdraw everything except any scholarship processes already in flight that you want to complete for closure (actually, withdraw those too; ED means done). Celebrate. Skip to Apr 2028.
  - **Deferred:** Hopkins moves to their RD pool. Send RD apps and a brief update letter to Hopkins in Jan with any new developments.
  - **Denied:** Decide within days on WashU ED2 (deadline typically early Jan; verify scholarship compatibility, checked back in Sept). Submit all RD apps by their deadlines.
- Late Dec: Purdue, IU, and possibly UIUC EA results arrive. She should have at least one affordable admit in hand before New Year's Eve.

**Jan 2028**
- Jan 1-5: RD deadlines for Harvard, Stanford, Duke, Northwestern, WashU (or WashU ED2). Submit whatever is still live.
- Mid-year grades sent by the counselor. Keep senior grades strong; ED admits get rescinded for collapses, and RD schools read the mid-year report.
- Case EA decision typically arrives this window; merit offers may come with it or later.

**Feb 2028**
- Quiet month. Interviews if offered (Case values them; some reaches offer alumni interviews). Scholarship finalist rounds (Wells, WashU programs, Robertson if invited) may involve interviews or visits.
- Keep the spreadsheet updated as EA merit offers arrive.

**Mar 2028**
- Late March: RD decisions arrive in a cluster.
- Build the final comparison spreadsheet (see Negotiation Guide). Send any merit reconsideration emails to Case and USC once competing offers are in hand.

**Apr 2028**
- Admitted student visits for the two or three finalists. She should sit in on a class and, if possible, talk to undergrads doing research.
- The family conversation: the four-year delta versus the med school fund, on one page.
- Deposit by May 1.

**May 2028**
- May 1: National Decision Day. Deposit at one school, decline the rest promptly (someone on a waitlist is refreshing their email).
- AP exams. Finish strong; her scores may earn credit or placement.
- Thank-you notes to recommenders and the counselor, with the outcome. Teachers remember the kids who close the loop.

## External Scholarships

The honest framing first: for your family, external scholarships are a modest supplement, not a strategy. Two structural reasons. First, most external awards are need-based or need-influenced, which screens you out. Second, at schools meeting full need this doesn't matter to you (you have no need-based aid to displace), but note that external awards simply reduce your bill dollar-for-dollar at full-pay prices, which is exactly what you want. The math works better for you than for aided families. The catch is that the awards themselves are competitive and small relative to your bill.

### Worth Real Effort

- **National Merit** (already in the plan). The highest-value "external" money available to her, via the October 2026 PSAT. USC's $28,000+/year for Finalists is the headline; Finalist status also unlocks awards at many schools.
- **Coca-Cola Scholars Program.** Large national award, merit-based, no income screen, and her profile (research, service, 12-year commitment) is the kind it rewards. Application opens in the fall of senior year. Long odds, short application relative to the payoff.
- **Regeneron Science Talent Search.** Only if the research placement produces a genuine independent project by fall of senior year. This is as much an admissions credential as a scholarship; even semifinalist status is significant. Application due Nov of senior year.
- **U.S. Presidential Scholars Program.** Not money, but a recognition that follows from top test scores; no separate effort until invited.
- **School-specific named scholarships** (WashU's programs, Duke's Robertson, IU's Wells, Case's Squire and A.W. Smith, USC's Trustee). Technically internal, but they belong on the same calendar and represent her best dollars-per-hour by far. These are already in the plan above.

### Worth a Targeted Search (Types, Not Names)

Do not trust any list I could generate for local awards; they change yearly. Instead, search these categories:

- **Naperville-area community foundation awards** (the DuPage County area has active community foundations with scholarship programs; many are merit or activity-based rather than need-based).
- **Hospital auxiliary and medical association scholarships.** Edward Hospital's volunteer program or auxiliary may have awards for volunteers pursuing medicine. Ask her volunteer coordinator directly; this is her single best local lead.
- **Parent employer programs.** Both of you work for substantial companies. Check whether either employer (the medical device company especially) offers dependent scholarships or STEM awards. Five minutes with HR.
- **Indian-American community and cultural organization scholarships**, national and Chicago-area. Several reward exactly her combination of academics and classical arts training.
- **Science journalism or student writing awards.** Her column makes her eligible for student journalism recognitions that most research kids can't touch.
- **Rotary, Lions, and similar civic club awards** in Naperville. Small dollars, low competition, and they like hospital volunteers.

### Where to Search

1. **The Naperville North counseling office's scholarship list.** Every high school maintains one, updated with local deadlines. This is the highest-yield source for local awards and Priya should check it monthly starting junior spring.
2. **Illinois Student Assistance Commission** (isac.org) for state programs, mostly to confirm what doesn't apply to you.
3. **Scholarships360, Going Merry, and Fastweb** for national databases. Create profiles once, filter aggressively for merit-only awards with no income requirement, and ignore anything requiring an essay for under $1,000.
4. **Each college's own scholarship page**, senior fall, for institutional awards with separate applications.

### Time-Investment Analysis

Be clear-eyed with her about this, because she's exactly the kid who will spreadsheet forty scholarships and apply to all of them out of completionism:

- **National Merit prep:** already in the plan, best ROI available.
- **The big nationals (Coca-Cola, Regeneron):** maybe 10-15 hours each, low odds, but high value and the materials recycle from her college essays. Worth it.
- **Institutional named scholarships:** the best dollars-per-hour on this list. Prioritize these over everything below.
- **Local awards:** typically $500-$2,500 each, but odds are dramatically better than nationals. A focused 15-20 hours in winter of senior year across 5-8 local applications is reasonable and might return a few thousand dollars.
- **Mass-applying on the national databases:** poor ROI for a full-pay family chasing $500 awards against thousands of applicants. Cap it.

Total sanctioned budget: roughly 40 hours across senior year, most of it recycled essay material, aimed at National Merit mechanics, two big nationals, the institutional awards, and a handful of local applications. If she's spending hour 41, redirect her to her AP Physics homework or, better, to doing nothing at all for an evening. She's earned it, and by then she will have more than earned it.
