submissions.output:
# Brett's College Strategy
*Boca Raton, FL | Rising 12th Grade | Scaffold July 2026*

## Executive Summary

Brett is exactly the kind of applicant engineering programs want: a 3.9 unweighted GPA at Boca Raton Community High, a 1450 PSAT pointing toward a 1500+ SAT, AP Calc BC and AP Physics already on the transcript, three years of VEX robotics with a shot at captain, and, my favorite detail in your entire intake, a mechanical keyboard business he actually runs and profits from. He builds things. That's the story, and it's a good one. His gaps are real but manageable: writing is functional rather than strong (which matters for essays, so we start early), and he procrastinates on long-term projects (which is exactly what a college application is).

Your financial picture drives this strategy as much as his academics. At $200K income with $300K in home equity, you will qualify for little to no need-based aid anywhere. The elite no-merit schools (MIT, Stanford) will cost you roughly $47,000-$50,500 per year, which is $7,000-$10,000 over your $40K budget. Meanwhile, Florida Bright Futures plus in-state tuition makes UF, UCF, and FAU cost around $13,000-$14,500 per year. That spread, $13K versus $50K for four years, is the central decision your family will face in April 2027.

| # | School | Tier | Round | Est. Net Cost/Year | Financial Risk |
|---|--------|------|-------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | MIT* | Reach | EA | ~$46,500 | High |
| 2 | Stanford* | Reach | RD | ~$50,500 | Very High |
| 3 | Georgia Tech* | Reach | EA | ~$46,500 | High |
| 4 | Case Western Reserve* | Target | EA | ~$49,000 | High |
| 5 | Purdue | Target | EA | ~$40,500 | Moderate |
| 6 | University of Florida* | Target | Nov 1 deadline | ~$12,700 | Very Low |
| 7 | University of Central Florida | Safety | Rolling (apply Aug-Sept) | ~$14,500 | Very Low |
| 8 | Florida Atlantic University | Safety | Rolling (apply Aug-Sept) | ~$13,700 | Very Low |
| 9 | University of Alabama | Safety | Rolling (apply by early fall) | ~$15,700 | Very Low |

*Schools marked with asterisk were on your radar.

**Top 3 things to do in the next 6 months:**
1. **Lock the SAT score.** If Brett doesn't already have a 1500+ from a spring sitting, register today for the August 29, 2026 SAT. MIT, Georgia Tech, and the Florida publics all require scores. This one number moves his odds and his merit money more than anything else he can do this summer.
2. **Draft the Common App essay in July, before senior year starts.** Writing is his weaker muscle and he procrastinates. A builder-narrative essay drafted now, revised in August, done by Labor Day, removes the biggest failure mode in this plan.
3. **Verify Bright Futures hours and file the paperwork.** Florida Academic Scholars requires 100 hours of community service or paid work. His bagel shop job likely covers it, but the hours must be documented and submitted through your school. Confirm with his counselor in August, and file the Florida Financial Aid Application (FFAA) in the fall.

**The financial floor:** Florida Atlantic University, about 20 minutes from your house, with near-certain admission and Bright Futures covering tuition: roughly **$13,700 per year**, and its Wilkes Honors College is realistic for his stats. UCF (~$14,500) and Alabama with automatic merit (~$15,700) sit right beside it. The worst case here is a strong engineering education at a third of your budget. That is a genuinely good worst case, and it's what lets us swing at MIT and Stanford without anxiety.

## The Four Threads

Every strong application has a spine. Brett's is not "well-rounded kid," it's **"the kid who builds things and ships them."** Four threads, one story:

**1. The Builder.** Three years of VEX robotics, 3D printing, mechanical design. If he becomes captain senior year, that's leadership layered on top of genuine technical depth. His essays and activity descriptions should be full of specifics: mechanisms he designed, problems he debugged at 11pm before a competition.

**2. The Builder Who Sells.** The mechanical keyboard business is the differentiator. Thousands of applicants do robotics. Very few design, manufacture, and sell a physical product online. This shows engineering judgment plus initiative plus follow-through, and it's the thread admissions readers will remember. He should be able to talk revenue, iteration, customer feedback. Do not let him undersell this as "a hobby."

**3. Quantitative Firepower.** 3.9 unweighted, Calc BC and Physics 1 as a junior, and a physics teacher who says he asks the questions she wishes the whole class would ask. That teacher should write one of his recommendations, full stop. That sentence, in her voice, is worth more than any award. The second letter should come from a math teacher who's seen the same speed.

**4. Work Ethic Without Drama.** Varsity tennis, math honor society, and a weekend job at a bagel shop. The job matters more than you might think: admissions officers consistently value real work, and it doubles as his Bright Futures hours. "Driven but laid back about it" is a great temperament for engineering school, and it should come through in his voice, not be claimed outright.

**What not to do:** Don't add anything new. No new clubs, no summer resume-padding program, no volunteering sprint invented in August. His core is strong and coherent. The remaining work is deepening (robotics captaincy, keyboard business documentation) and executing (essays, deadlines).

## School List

A quick note on early-round strategy before the list. Stanford uses Restrictive Early Action, which would forbid Brett from applying EA to MIT, Case Western, or any other private school. We are **not** recommending Stanford REA, because MIT is the better-fit school for a hands-on mechanical/aerospace builder, MIT's EA is non-restrictive, and Stanford's REA boost for an unhooked applicant is modest. So: MIT EA, Stanford RD, and everything else EA or rolling. No school on this list is forced into a round it doesn't want.

### 1. MIT (Reach, on your radar)

- **Verified numbers:** 4.5% admit rate, SAT mid-50% 1520-1570. Sticker $85,994. Net price for your income bracket: $46,627. Meets 100% of need. **No merit aid.**
- **Admission odds:** MIT's overall admit rate is 4.5%. Brett's realistic odds are about 5%. His profile is exactly what MIT looks for (maker, builder, quantitative), but that describes most of MIT's applicant pool. Nothing about this is a criticism of Brett; it's just base-rate honesty.
- **Cost reality:** At $200K income plus home equity, expect roughly **$46,500/year**, about $6,500 over budget. MIT gives zero merit aid, so there is no path to bringing this down. Over four years, that's roughly $26K beyond budget, and your $60K 529 covers a bit over one year's cost. Doable with a stretch, and MIT's outcomes arguably justify it. That's your call to make in April, not now.
- **Round: EA (Nov 1).** Non-restrictive, decision by mid-December, no downside.
- **Logistics:** CSS Profile required. Does not track demonstrated interest. Tests required; submit the 1500+. MIT has its own application (not Common App) with short essays, which actually favors Brett: punchy and specific over lyrical.
- **Action items:** MIT's essays reward the maker identity. The keyboard business and VEX should anchor his "pleasure" and "contribution" prompts. Start these in August.

### 2. Stanford (Reach, on your radar)

- **Verified numbers:** 3.6% admit rate, SAT mid-50% 1510-1570. Sticker $90,711. Net price for your bracket: $50,452. Meets 100% of need. **No merit aid** (a fraction of a percent of students get small awards; do not plan around it).
- **Admission odds:** Stanford's overall admit rate is 3.6%, and for an unhooked applicant, Brett's odds are essentially that base rate, call it 3.6%. Buy the lottery ticket if he wants it; just price it as a lottery ticket.
- **Cost reality:** Roughly **$50,500/year**, about $10,500 over budget every year. The most expensive option on this list, and there's no merit lever. This is why the financial risk rating is Very High.
- **Round: RD (early January).** Applying REA would kill MIT EA and Case EA, and the strategic math doesn't favor that trade. RD costs Brett little here.
- **Logistics:** CSS Profile required (home equity counts, see Financial Aid Strategy). Does not track demonstrated interest. Tests required for his cycle.
- **Honest note:** If in December you're looking at this application and the essays aren't done, cutting Stanford is a fine outcome. It's the weakest fit-per-dollar on the list and it's here because you asked about it, which is a perfectly good reason.

### 3. Georgia Tech (Reach, on your radar)

- **Verified numbers:** 16.5% overall admit rate, SAT mid-50% 1330-1530. Out-of-state sticker $47,636. The published net price for your bracket ($17,250) is misleading for you: it's dominated by in-state Georgia students. As an out-of-state family with no demonstrated need, expect close to sticker.
- **Admission odds:** 16.5% overall, but Georgia Tech admits out-of-state applicants at a meaningfully lower rate, and engineering is its most competitive door. Realistic estimate for Brett: **~12%**. His stats are at the very top of the range, and the builder narrative is a genuine fit for GT's culture, so this is a reach worth taking seriously.
- **Cost reality:** Roughly **$46,500/year** out-of-state with minimal aid. The **Stamps President's Scholars Program** covers full cost of attendance plus enrichment funding, but it's among the most competitive scholarships in the country. Apply, don't count on it. Also note: Florida offers ABET-accredited aerospace and mechanical engineering in-state, so the SREB Academic Common Market will not get you in-state rates at GT.
- **Round: EA (Nov 1 deadline for non-Georgia residents).** Decision in late January. Non-binding, and GT gives no ED option anyway.
- **Logistics:** FAFSA only (public). Does not track demonstrated interest. Tests required.
- **The uncomfortable math:** GT is arguably the best pure-engineering fit of the reaches, and it costs the same as MIT for you. If Brett gets into both GT and UF, you'll be weighing a $33K/year difference for a real but not enormous difference in engineering outcomes. Worth discussing as a family before decisions arrive, not after.

### 4. Case Western Reserve (Target, on your radar)

- **Verified numbers:** 37.8% admit rate, SAT mid-50% 1450-1530. Sticker $89,514. Net price for your bracket: $49,117. Meets 100% of need. Merit: 30.7% of students receive it, averaging $30,499.
- **Admission odds:** 37.8% overall; with Brett's stats at the median of Case's admitted range and a strong STEM narrative, call it **~42%**. A true target.
- **Cost reality:** Here's the catch. Case is generous with merit, and Brett is a strong candidate for a $25K-$35K award. But the verified net price for families in your bracket, which already bakes in typical merit, is $49,117. Expect roughly **$49,000/year**, about $9,000 over budget, even in the likely scenario where he gets merit. The named full-tuition awards, the **Andrew and Eleanor Squire Scholarship** and the **A.W. Smith Innovation Scholarship** (~$69,000/year), would change everything, and the Smith award in particular was built for a kid like Brett. Apply for them; don't budget around them. National Merit Finalists get an additional $1,000-$2,000 if Case is their first choice.
- **Round: EA (Nov 1).** Case's ED rate (37.1%) is actually no better than its overall rate, so ED buys nothing and costs you the ability to compare offers. EA is clearly right.
- **Logistics:** CSS Profile required. **Case tracks demonstrated interest and it matters there.** Brett should join the mailing list, attend a virtual info session, email the regional admissions officer with a genuine question, and visit campus if you're ever near Cleveland. This is the one school on the list where those clicks and visits move the needle.

### 5. Purdue (Target)

- **Verified numbers:** 49.9% overall admit rate, SAT mid-50% 1210-1470. Out-of-state sticker roughly $42,444 all-in. Merit: 15.3% receive, averaging $5,800. As with GT, published net-price figures are skewed by in-state students; expect near sticker minus a possible modest merit award.
- **Why it's here:** You didn't name it, but a mechanical/aero kid's list without Purdue has a hole in it. Top-tier aerospace and mechanical engineering, enormous undergraduate research operation, and one of the most reasonable out-of-state price tags among elite engineering publics.
- **Admission odds:** 49.9% overall, but first-year engineering is the competitive door. Realistic estimate: **~45%**.
- **Cost reality:** Roughly **$40,500/year**, essentially at your budget, with a decent shot at a few thousand in merit that would nudge it under. This is your "great engineering school at budget" option, sitting between the $47K reaches and the $13K Florida schools.
- **Round: EA (Nov 1).** Non-binding, and EA is effectively required for scholarship and honors consideration.
- **Logistics:** FAFSA only. Doesn't track demonstrated interest. The **John Martinson Honors College** requires a separate application; with Brett's stats (honors average SAT 1409), he's a strong candidate. Do it.

### 6. University of Florida (Target, on your radar)

- **Verified numbers:** 24.2% overall admit rate, SAT mid-50% 1330-1470. In-state sticker $18,996. Net price for your bracket: $15,571 before Bright Futures.
- **Admission odds:** 24.2% overall, but UF is far friendlier to in-state applicants, and Brett's projected 1500 sits above UF's 75th percentile with a 3.9 unweighted. Realistic estimate: **~45%**. Not a lock (UF denies strong Florida kids every year, and engineering is competitive), which is why UCF and FAU back it up.
- **Cost reality:** With Bright Futures Florida Academic Scholars covering 100% of tuition and fees plus $300/semester, your cost is essentially housing, food, and books: roughly **$12,700/year**. Under a third of budget. Four years at UF costs less than one year at Stanford.
- **The Honors angle:** UF's Honors Program admits about 13% of applicants (roughly 2,000 of 16,000), typically 1400+ SAT, via an extra essay in the Common App. Brett should absolutely apply; honors admits become eligible for the Lombardi and Stamps Scholarships. And the wild card: if Brett makes **National Merit Finalist**, Florida's **Benacquisto Scholarship** covers his full cost of attendance at UF, making it literally free. His 1450 PSAT puts him near Florida's Semifinalist cutoff; you'll find out in September 2026. Don't plan on it, but know what it means if the letter comes.
- **Round: Apply by the Nov 1 deadline** (UF has a single deadline with decisions in late winter). Treat it like an EA deadline.
- **Logistics:** FAFSA only. Doesn't track demonstrated interest. Test scores required (Florida publics never went test-optional).

### 7. University of Central Florida (Safety)

- **Verified numbers:** 39.5% admit rate, SAT mid-50% 1190-1350. In-state sticker $20,438. Net price for your bracket: $17,821 before Bright Futures.
- **Admission odds:** Brett's stats sit far above UCF's 75th percentile. The overall rate understates an in-state applicant with his numbers; I'll estimate conservatively at **~60%**, and in practice I'd be shocked by a denial. Rolling admissions means he can apply in August and likely have an acceptance in hand by early fall, which does wonders for everyone's blood pressure.
- **Why it's here:** UCF has legitimate mechanical and aerospace engineering with deep ties to the Space Coast industry corridor (Lockheed, L3Harris, NASA-adjacent contractors recruit heavily there). For an aero kid, this is not a consolation prize.
- **Cost reality:** With Bright Futures FAS covering tuition and fees: roughly **$14,500/year**.
- **Round: Rolling. Apply in August-September 2026.**
- **Logistics:** FAFSA only. Apply to the Honors College as well.

### 8. Florida Atlantic University (Safety)

- **Verified numbers:** 73.4% admit rate, SAT mid-50% 1040-1210. In-state sticker $19,415. Net price for your bracket: $16,235 before Bright Futures.
- **Admission odds:** **~90%+**. Brett's numbers are roughly 300 points above FAU's 75th percentile.
- **Why it's here:** It's your hometown school, it has an engineering college, and its **Wilkes Honors College** (average SAT 1295) would treat Brett as a star recruit, with small classes and priority research access. Living at home is also an option that drops the cost further, though I'd generally recommend the residential experience.
- **Cost reality:** With Bright Futures: roughly **$13,700/year**, the cheapest number on this list. **This is your financial floor.**
- **Round: Rolling. Apply in August-September 2026**, and to Wilkes Honors.

### 9. University of Alabama (Safety)

- **Verified numbers:** 76.6% admit rate. Out-of-state sticker $47,688. Merit: 83.4% of students receive it, averaging $19,109.
- **Why it's here:** Alabama runs one of the most aggressive automatic merit programs in the country for out-of-state students. With a 3.9 GPA and a 1500 SAT, Brett clears the published thresholds for automatic full-tuition-level scholarships. No competition, no essay contest: hit the numbers, get the money. And if he makes National Merit Finalist and names Alabama first choice, the package becomes tuition plus four years of housing plus a $4,000/year stipend plus a $2,000 research allowance, which is close to a full ride.
- **Admission odds:** **~95%**. His stats are far above the admitted range, and the merit thresholds are published.
- **Cost reality:** After automatic merit, roughly **$15,700/year** (housing, food, fees, books). Alabama's Honors College (separate application) is a given at his stats.
- **Round: Rolling, apply by early fall 2026** and check the scholarship priority deadline on Alabama's site. Merit money is tied to early application.
- **Honest note:** This is the "out-of-state adventure at in-state prices" option. Alabama's engineering program is solid and improving, though not at GT/Purdue's level. If Brett wants to leave Florida and you want to stay well under budget, this is how.

**Schools deliberately left off:** Embry-Riddle (aerospace brand, but ~$43K net for a program the Florida publics match at a third of the price), Florida Tech (same math), and additional elite privates like Duke or Michigan (they'd cost $47K+ like the existing reaches without adding a distinct option; three reaches at that price point is enough). No padding.

## Financial Aid Strategy

**The blunt version:** you said it yourself, "we make too much for need-based aid but $85K a year is not happening." You're right on both counts, and this list is built around that. Your aid strategy is merit and Bright Futures, not need.

**Florida Bright Futures, Florida Academic Scholars (FAS).** This is an entitlement program: meet the criteria, get the money, no competition. Requirements for Brett's class: 3.5 weighted GPA in the 16 core courses (his 3.9 unweighted clears this easily), 1330 SAT or 29 ACT (his projected 1500 clears it with room to spare, and even a bad test day lands above 1330), and **100 hours of community service or paid work**. That last one is the trap families miss. His bagel shop job should satisfy it, but the hours must be documented and logged with his school. **Confirm with his counselor in August 2026** that the hours are recorded, and file the **Florida Financial Aid Application (FFAA)** during senior year (deadline is August 31 after graduation, but do it in the fall). FAS covers 100% of tuition and applicable fees at Florida publics plus $300/semester. It's worth roughly $6,400+/year at UF, and it does not require staying near home: it works at UF, UCF, FAU, FSU, and other Florida institutions. Note: Bright Futures requires enrollment right after high school (August 31 deadline), so it's incompatible with an unplanned gap year.

**Benacquisto Scholarship.** If Brett is named a National Merit Finalist, Florida covers his **full cost of attendance** at a Florida public. September 2026 (Semifinalist announcements) is the moment to watch. His 1450 PSAT is close to Florida's historical cutoff; it depends on his exact selection index. If it happens, UF becomes free and the calculus for the whole list shifts. Also, several out-of-state schools on this list pay for NMF status: Alabama's package is the headline, and Case adds $1,000-$2,000.

**FAFSA and CSS Profile.** File the FAFSA when it opens **October 1, 2026**, using your 2025 tax return. Every school needs it, even though you won't qualify for grants; it's required for merit processing at some schools and keeps federal loan options open. MIT, Stanford, and Case Western also require the **CSS Profile**, and here's the part that surprises homeowner families: **the CSS Profile counts your ~$300K of home equity as an asset. FAFSA does not.** At your income, the FAFSA-only schools (all the publics on this list) were never going to give you need aid anyway, so this mostly means the CSS schools' calculators will confirm what the verified net prices already show: roughly $46K-$50K per year at MIT, Stanford, and Case. When you run their Net Price Calculators, enter the home equity honestly so you're not surprised in March.

**QuestBridge:** not applicable to your family; it serves households generally under ~$65K income.

**Run the Net Price Calculators this summer** at all nine schools, even the obvious ones. It takes an evening and it's the single best pre-application financial reality check available.

**The 529 math:** $60K covers roughly four to five years at the Florida-school price point, or about 1.2 years at the MIT/Stanford price point. Also worth remembering: your daughter is 13 years old and four years behind Brett. Whatever you spend over budget on Brett's college is money that isn't available when her turn comes in 2030. Two kids at $50K/year overlapping for a year or two is a very different proposition than two kids at $15K/year.

## Summer Plan (Summer 2026, Happening Now)

This is the last summer before applications, and it's already July, so this is a six-week execution plan, not an aspiration list.

**July:**
- **Common App essay, first draft.** Given his writing and procrastination profile, this is the priority. The strongest topic is sitting right there: the keyboard business or a specific robotics build, told through one concrete moment. Set a real deadline: full draft by July 31. What not to do: don't let him write a generic "I love engineering" essay, and don't frame anything around hardship he hasn't had. Specific, technical, a little funny is his lane.
- **Run all nine Net Price Calculators** (parents' job, one evening).
- **SAT check.** If he has a 1500+ from spring, done. If not, register now for August 29, 2026.
- **Keep the bagel job.** It's Bright Futures hours, it's a great essay detail, and it signals maturity.

**August:**
- **Applications to UCF, FAU, and Alabama go in as soon as they open.** Rolling admissions rewards speed, and Alabama's merit is tied to early filing. By October, Brett could have three affordable acceptances banked before the hard apps are even due.
- **Robotics captain prep.** If captain selection happens in fall, he should spend August planning the season: recruiting underclassmen, setting a build schedule. If he becomes captain, the application updates itself; if not, "design lead" with real output tells the same story.
- **Document the keyboard business.** Photos, units sold, revenue, design iterations. This becomes his Common App activity description and possibly a portfolio link (MIT's Maker Portfolio is worth considering).
- **Case Western demonstrated interest:** info session sign-up, mailing list, one genuine email to the regional rep.
- **Supplemental essay outlines** for MIT, GT, Case, Purdue, UF Honors, Stanford. He doesn't draft them all in August, but he should know the prompts before school starts.

## Application Timeline

- **August-September 2026:** Rolling apps out (UCF, FAU, Alabama). Essay revision. SAT August 29 if needed. NMSF announcement in September. Confirm Bright Futures hours. Ask the physics teacher and a math teacher for recommendations the first week of school.
- **October 1, 2026:** FAFSA opens; file it. CSS Profile for MIT, Stanford, Case. FFAA for Bright Futures.
- **By November 1, 2026:** Submit MIT EA, Georgia Tech EA, Case Western EA, Purdue EA, and UF (with the Honors essay).
- **December 2026:** MIT EA decision mid-month. Rolling decisions should already be in hand.
- **Early January 2027:** Stanford RD. If December went well, this is the only application left.
- **January-March 2027:** GT (late January), Case, Purdue, UF (late February), Stanford (late March).
- **April 2027:** Compare offers with the decision framework below. Deposit by May 1.

## Probability and Cost Estimates

These are informed estimates based on Brett's profile against verified admission and cost data. They are not outputs of a statistical simulation, and no one, including us, can predict a 3.6% admit rate school.

**Headline stats:**
- Probability Brett has **at least one acceptance at or under $16K/year**: ~99% (three near-certain safeties, all under budget).
- Probability of **at least one acceptance at a top-tier engineering program** (MIT, Stanford, GT, Case, Purdue, UF): roughly 85%, driven mostly by Purdue, UF, and Case.
- Probability of an MIT or Stanford acceptance: under 10% combined. Real, not likely.

**The April 2027 decision scenarios:**

1. **MIT says yes (~5%):** You're deciding whether ~$46,500/year, about $126K over budget across four years, is worth MIT. There's no wrong answer, but decide with your daughter's timeline in view.
2. **GT and/or Case yes, MIT/Stanford no (~35-40%):** The most interesting scenario. GT at ~$46,500 or Case at ~$49,000 versus UF at ~$12,700. Unless Case comes through with a Squire or Smith full-tuition award, the honest engineering-outcomes answer is that UF Honors plus strong internships closes most of the gap at a $135K four-year discount.
3. **Purdue yes, reaches no (~30%):** Purdue at ~$40,500 versus UF at ~$12,700. Purdue's aero program is a real step up; whether it's a $110K step up is your family's call.
4. **UF Honors plus Benacquisto (if NMF):** UF becomes free and this whole document simplifies to one paragraph.
5. **Florida schools only (~10-15%):** UF, UCF, or FAU with honors, at $13-15K/year. Still a strong engineering path with money left over for grad school, which matters in aero.

## What If the Profile Lands Lower

Suppose the SAT comes back 1400-1450 instead of 1500+, or first-semester senior grades dip, or the captaincy doesn't happen. Here's the honest picture, and it's reassuring:

- **Bright Futures FAS is safe.** The threshold is 1330 SAT and a 3.5 weighted GPA. Even a bad test day clears it. Your $13-15K floor at UF/UCF/FAU does not move.
- **UCF, FAU, and Alabama don't change.** All three admit him comfortably at 1400. Alabama's merit tiers are score-dependent, so the award might drop a notch; the net cost rises a few thousand but stays well under budget.
- **UF gets tighter.** At 1400, his odds drop from ~45% toward ~30%, and UF Honors becomes a stretch. Still worth applying; UCF covers the downside.
- **Case Western:** 1400 is below Case's 25th percentile (1450). Case is test-optional, so he'd apply without scores, leaning on the 3.9 GPA and the builder narrative. Merit becomes less likely, which honestly matters little since Case was over budget even with merit.
- **MIT, Stanford, GT:** All require scores. At 1400, these move from long shots to non-starters, and the right call is to cut them and save the essay hours. Purdue becomes the top of the list, and it's a terrific top.
- **If the captaincy doesn't happen:** No structural change. The keyboard business carries the initiative story on its own, and "design lead who mentored the new members" reads nearly as well as "captain."

The plan degrades gracefully. In every downside scenario, Brett still ends up an engineer at a good program you can afford without borrowing.

## What to Do Now

1. **SAT decision this week.** Confirm the existing score or register for August 29, 2026.
2. **Common App essay draft by July 31.** Parents: your job is the deadline, not the editing. His procrastination is the single biggest risk in this plan, and it's solvable in July, not November.
3. **Run all nine Net Price Calculators** this month, entering home equity honestly at the CSS schools.
4. **Confirm Bright Futures work hours are documented** with the counselor when school opens, and calendar the FFAA.
5. **File the rolling applications (UCF, FAU, Alabama) in August-September** so Brett walks into the hard deadlines with acceptances already in hand.

submissions.output:
# Brett's College Strategy
*Boca Raton, FL | 11th Grade (rising senior) | Scaffold July 2026*

## Executive Summary

Brett is applying this fall, so this document is a launch plan, not a long-range roadmap. Here's his projected profile: 3.9 unweighted GPA at Boca Raton Community High School with a rigorous STEM load (AP Calc BC, AP Physics 1, AP Lang completed junior year), a 1450 PSAT pointing toward a 1500+ SAT, three years of VEX robotics with a captain bid senior year, varsity tennis, and, the thing that makes him genuinely interesting to admissions readers, a real small business building and selling mechanical keyboards. His gap: writing is functional but not a strength, and the essays will decide whether the reaches are live or not. His strengths: quantitative firepower, demonstrated hands-on building, and a teacher who says he asks the questions she wishes the whole class would ask. That last line belongs in a recommendation letter, and we'll get it there.

Your financial reality is the spine of this plan: $200K income, home equity, minimal need-based aid anywhere, $40K/year budget, $60K in the 529. Florida Bright Futures makes in-state schools almost free for Brett. Merit money is the only lever out of state.

| # | School | Tier | Round | Est. Net Cost/Yr | Financial Risk |
|---|--------|------|-------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Florida Atlantic University | Safety | Rolling (apply Sept) | $13,000-14,000 | Very Low |
| 2 | University of Central Florida | Safety | Rolling (apply Sept-Oct) | $14,000-15,000 | Very Low |
| 3 | University of Florida* | Target | Nov 1 deadline (single round) | $12,000-14,000 | Very Low |
| 4 | University of Alabama | Safety | Rolling (apply Aug-Sept) | $20,000-26,000 | Low |
| 5 | Purdue University | Target | EA (Nov 1) | $31,000-34,000 | Low |
| 6 | Georgia Tech* | Reach | EA2 (Nov) | $44,000-48,000 | High |
| 7 | MIT* | Reach | EA (Nov 1) | ~$47,000 | High |
| 8 | Case Western Reserve* | Target | EA (Nov 1) | $45,000-51,000 | Very High |
| 9 | Stanford* | Reach | RD (early Jan) | ~$50,000 | Very High |

*Schools marked with asterisk were on your radar.

**Top 3 things to do in the next 6 months:**
1. **Lock the SAT in August 2026.** Register for the August test date now, with October as backup. MIT and Georgia Tech require scores, UF has always required them, and Stanford requires them for Brett's cycle. Everything hinges on converting that 1450 PSAT into a 1500+.
2. **Verify Bright Futures hours this month.** Florida Academic Scholars requires 100 hours of community service or paid work for Brett's class. His bagel shop job likely counts toward this, but the hours must be documented and approved through his counselor. Call the school in early August. Do not let a paperwork gap cost you 100% of in-state tuition.
3. **Draft essays before school starts.** Brett procrastinates on long-term projects (your words), and essays are the ultimate long-term project. The Common App personal statement and the MIT short answers should have full drafts by the end of August, before robotics and tennis eat his fall.

**The financial floor:** Florida Atlantic University, admission near-certain, roughly $13,500/year after Bright Futures Florida Academic Scholars, with the Wilkes Honors College well within reach given his stats. That's the worst-case scenario, and it's a genuinely good one: a solid engineering degree twenty minutes from home at a third of your budget. Everything above it is upside.

## The Four Threads

Every strong application tells one coherent story. Brett's story writes itself if we let it: **he's a builder who ships.** Not a kid who joined the robotics club, but a kid who designs, prototypes, manufactures, and sells physical products. Four threads:

**1. The keyboard business.** This is the crown jewel and I want to be blunt about why. Thousands of applicants to MIT and Georgia Tech have robotics and AP Physics. Almost none of them run a real business making mechanical products with actual customers, actual revenue, and actual quality control problems. This goes first in the activities list, and it's the personal statement. The essay isn't "I like keyboards." It's the moment a batch failed, or a customer complained, or he had to redesign a plate because the tolerances were off. That essay demonstrates engineering thinking better than any award could.

**2. Robotics leadership.** Three years of VEX with a captain bid senior year (aim for it, but don't write it as done until it happens). The goal for fall: a specific, nameable contribution as a leader. Not "led the team," but the drivetrain redesign, the scouting system, the way he taught underclassmen CAD. Depth, not titles.

**3. Quantitative rigor.** AP Calc BC and AP Physics 1 junior year, and senior year should include AP Physics C if Boca High offers it, plus the most rigorous math available. Admissions readers evaluate rigor relative to his school. He should be in the top handful of course loads Boca Raton Community High offers. His counselor's "most demanding" checkbox matters at MIT and Stanford.

**4. The balance signals.** Varsity tennis and a weekend job at a bagel shop. Don't underrate these. The job especially reads as maturity and work ethic, and it may literally fund his Bright Futures eligibility (paid work hours count). Keep both. Add nothing new. His profile doesn't need another club; it needs the existing four threads to go deeper.

**Recommenders:** the physics teacher who said he asks the right questions is recommender one, full stop. Ask her before school ends its first week. Recommender two should be a humanities teacher (AP Lang is the obvious source) who can vouch that the quantitative kid can also communicate, which preempts the "writing isn't his strength" concern.

## School List

### 1. Florida Atlantic University — Safety, Rolling (apply September)

- **Sticker (in-state):** $19,415 | **Verified net price ($110K+ bracket):** $16,235 | **Your estimate with Bright Futures FAS:** $13,000-14,000
- **CSS Profile:** No, FAFSA only | **Tracks demonstrated interest:** Not meaningfully

FAU's overall admit rate is 73.4%, and with Brett's stats I'd put him around 78%. It's not glamorous, and that's fine, because it does the one job a financial floor has to do: guarantee a good outcome at a price that doesn't hurt. Bright Futures FAS covers 100% of tuition and fees, leaving mostly room and board. The Wilkes Honors College (average SAT 1295, Brett projects 200+ points above that) would give him a small-cohort experience inside a big public. **Action:** apply in September, apply to Wilkes Honors, done by October.

### 2. University of Central Florida — Safety, Rolling (apply September-October)

- **Sticker (in-state):** $20,438 | **Verified net price ($110K+ bracket):** $17,821 | **Your estimate with FAS:** $14,000-15,000
- **CSS Profile:** No | **Tracks demonstrated interest:** No

UCF's overall admit rate is 39.5%, which surprises people who think of it as an easy admit. It isn't anymore. With Brett's profile I'd estimate around 44%. Why it's on the list anyway: UCF's engineering ties to the Space Coast are real, aerospace and mechanical engineering are strengths, and internship pipelines to the launch industry an hour away are among the best in the country for a kid who loves building things and Formula 1. **Action:** apply early in the rolling cycle when admit odds are best.

### 3. University of Florida* — Target, Single Deadline (Nov 1)

- **Sticker (in-state):** $18,996 | **Verified net price ($110K+ bracket):** $15,571 | **Your estimate with FAS:** $12,000-14,000
- **CSS Profile:** No | **Tracks demonstrated interest:** No

UF's overall admit rate is 24.2%. That's the headline number and I want you to sit with it, because a lot of strong Florida families assume UF is a safety and get burned. For an in-state applicant with a 3.9/1500 profile, I'd estimate roughly 28%, better than baseline but nowhere near certain. UF does not offer EA; there's one Nov 1 deadline with decisions in late winter. **The upside if he gets in is enormous:** a top-30 public engineering program at $12-14K/year after Bright Futures. Apply to the Honors Program (13% admit rate, requires the honors essay in the Common App, typically 1400+ SAT, and opens eligibility for additional scholarship consideration). **One wildcard:** if Brett's junior-year PSAT selection index makes him a National Merit Semifinalist (announced September 2026), the Benacquisto Scholarship covers full cost of attendance at Florida publics for Finalists. A 1450 PSAT puts him in the conversation depending on his section split. Don't plan on it; do watch for the notification. **Action:** complete the application including honors essay by mid-October, don't wait for the deadline.

### 4. University of Alabama — Safety, Rolling (apply August-September)

- **Sticker (OOS):** $47,688 | **Verified net price ($110K+ bracket):** $25,773 | **Your estimate after automatic merit:** $20,000-26,000
- **CSS Profile:** No | **Tracks demonstrated interest:** No

Overall admit rate is 76.6%; call Brett roughly 80%. Alabama runs one of the most aggressive merit programs in the country: 83.4% of freshmen receive merit aid averaging $19,109, and out-of-state students with high GPA and test scores qualify for automatic full-tuition-level scholarships based on published thresholds Brett will clear comfortably. Aerospace and mechanical engineering are legitimate there, and the Honors College (average SAT 1360) would be his cohort. If he lands National Merit Finalist, Alabama's package is extraordinary: tuition plus four years of housing plus a $4,000/year stipend and $2,000 research allowance (first-choice designation required). Tuscaloosa is a true college town, not rural isolation. **Action:** apply in August when the application opens. Scholarship consideration is tied to early application, and having an acceptance with a scholarship number in hand by October changes the whole family's stress level.

### 5. Purdue University — Target, Early Action (Nov 1)

- **Sticker (OOS):** $38,824 | **Your estimate:** $31,000-34,000
- **CSS Profile:** No, FAFSA only | **Tracks demonstrated interest:** No

Purdue's overall admit rate is 49.9%, but first-year engineering is more selective than the university overall. For Brett, I'd estimate around 55% for the university with engineering being the real gate; treat it as a genuine target, not a safety. Here's why Purdue might be the single best value on this list: it is a top-10 engineering school nationally, arguably top-5 in aerospace (more astronauts than any other university, and the aero labs are the real thing), and out-of-state sticker is under your budget before any aid. Purdue has famously frozen tuition for over a decade. Merit is modest (15.3% receive, averaging $5,800), so budget for roughly $31-34K and treat any merit as a bonus. This is the school where "elite aero program" and "under $40K" actually intersect. **Action:** EA by Nov 1, non-binding, decision by mid-winter. Write the engineering supplemental around the keyboard business and robotics.

### 6. Georgia Tech* — Reach, Early Action 2 (November, non-Georgia deadline)

- **Sticker (OOS):** $47,636 | **Your estimate:** $44,000-48,000
- **CSS Profile:** Yes, for need-based aid (won't move much for you) | **Tracks demonstrated interest:** No

Overall admit rate is 16.5%, and out-of-state applicants face materially worse odds than Georgians. For an unhooked out-of-state applicant with Brett's profile, I'd estimate around 12%. Call it what it is: a reach, even for a strong kid. But it's the *right* reach. GT aerospace and mechanical engineering are elite, the co-op program is legendary, Atlanta fits his "nothing rural" requirement, and it leans Southeast like you do. Financially, expect close to sticker. The Scorecard net price you may see quoted (~$17K) is dominated by in-state Georgians on HOPE/Zell; that number is not you. The Stamps President's Scholars Program covers full cost of attendance and is spectacular, and also a lottery ticket at his profile level; apply by the early scholarship deadline but don't build the budget around it. At $44-48K, GT runs $4-8K/year over budget. Your 529 ($60K, roughly $15K/year over four years) covers that gap with room to spare, which is why I'm comfortable calling this High risk rather than a dealbreaker. **Action:** apply EA2 (the non-Georgia early round), and note GT requires test scores.

### 7. MIT* — Reach, Early Action (Nov 1)

- **Sticker:** $85,994 | **Verified net price ($110K+ bracket):** $46,627 | **Your estimate:** ~$47,000, possibly higher
- **CSS Profile:** Yes | **Tracks demonstrated interest:** No

Overall admit rate: 4.5%. For Brett, unhooked, with a strong-but-not-olympiad profile, I'd estimate around 5%. I'm not going to pretend the robotics-plus-business combination changes the base math much at a school where the applicant pool is wall-to-wall builders. What I will say: Brett is the *kind* of applicant MIT admits when it admits from this pool. The maker-with-a-business narrative, the physics teacher's line, the hands-on mechanical depth, these are MIT-shaped credentials. MIT offers zero merit aid; everything is need-based. The verified average net for $110K+ families is $46,627, but that bracket starts at $110K and you're at $200K with home equity, so run MIT's NPC expecting your number could land higher, potentially $55K+. **The strategic centerpiece: MIT's EA is non-restrictive.** Unlike Stanford's REA, applying MIT EA does not block any other early application. That's why MIT is the early anchor of this plan and Stanford is not (more below). MIT requires test scores. **Action:** EA by Nov 1, essays drafted by Labor Day, and the maker portfolio (photos and documentation of keyboard builds and robot iterations) ready for the application.

### 8. Case Western Reserve* — Target, Early Action (Nov 1)

- **Sticker:** $89,514 | **Verified net price ($110K+ bracket):** $49,117 | **Your estimate after likely merit:** $45,000-51,000
- **CSS Profile:** Yes | **Tracks demonstrated interest:** YES, actively

Overall admit rate is 37.8%; with Brett above the 1450-1530 middle-50 SAT range, I'd estimate around 42%. Admission is the easy part. The money is the hard part, and I need to be straight with you: Case is on your radar, and it's a wonderful engineering school with real undergrad research, but at your income the verified net price is $49,117/year. Merit is common there (30.7% of students, averaging $30,499), and Brett will very likely get a merit award, but the math already bakes that in: sticker is $89,514, so even $30-35K in merit plus a modest need-based award still leaves you around $45-51K. That's $5-11K/year over budget, every year. The paths that change this: the Andrew and Eleanor Squire Scholarship or the A.W. Smith Innovation Scholarship, both full tuition (~$69,000/year), both highly competitive. The A.W. Smith Innovation Scholarship in particular is aimed at exactly Brett's profile, an innovator-builder, and the keyboard business is a genuine differentiator there. Case also **tracks demonstrated interest heavily**: open every email, attend a virtual session, request contact with the regional admissions officer, visit if you're ever near Cleveland. Interest directly affects both admission and merit. **Action:** EA by Nov 1 (do NOT do ED; binding commitment at a school $5-11K over budget with your comparison-shopping strategy would be a mistake), pursue the full-tuition scholarships, and go in with clear eyes: standard merit probably isn't enough, and that's okay. If the full-tuition award doesn't come through, Purdue or GT likely wins this comparison.

### 9. Stanford* — Reach, Regular Decision (early January)

- **Sticker:** $90,711 | **Verified net price ($110K+ bracket):** $50,452 | **Your estimate:** ~$50,000, possibly higher
- **CSS Profile:** Yes | **Tracks demonstrated interest:** No

Overall admit rate: 3.6%. For Brett, I'd estimate around 4%. Now, the round decision, because this is where strategy matters. Stanford offers Restrictive Early Action, and REA would forbid Brett from applying early to *any* other private school. That means no MIT EA and no Case EA. I'm recommending against it. The REA bump at Stanford for unhooked applicants is modest, MIT is arguably the better fit for a mechanical builder anyway, and Case's EA round matters for merit consideration. Applying Stanford RD costs Brett very little and preserves the entire early calendar. Stanford offers no merit aid (the sub-1% who receive tiny awards are not a strategy). At $200K income plus $300K home equity, expect roughly $50K/year, possibly more once the CSS Profile counts your house; run the NPC. Stanford requires test scores for his cycle. **Action:** RD in early January, with essays that carry the same builder narrative. If the MIT EA decision arrives in December as a deferral or denial, the Stanford essays are already the backup swing.

**A note on what's NOT on this list.** No Embry-Riddle (aerospace brand, but ~$43K net for a program UF/UCF/Purdue beat on strength and price). No Michigan or Berkeley (out-of-state cost near $70-76K with negligible aid at your income, and I'm not going to tease you with schools that cost double your budget for marginal gain over GT). No additional Ivies, because you didn't ask for them and they'd all land at Stanford/MIT prices with worse engineering fit. Nine schools, every one with a reason to exist.

## QuestBridge

Not applicable to your family. QuestBridge serves households typically under $65K income. At $200K, Brett isn't eligible, and no time should be spent here. Mentioned only so you know it wasn't overlooked.

## Financial Aid Strategy

**Bright Futures is your foundation.** The Florida Academic Scholars (FAS) award covers 100% of tuition and applicable fees at Florida public institutions plus $300/semester. Requirements for Brett's class: 3.5 weighted GPA in the 16 core courses (he's comfortably there), 1330 SAT or 29 ACT (his 1450 PSAT says this is nearly done; the August SAT will formalize it), and 100 hours of community service or paid work. **This last one is the trap.** His bagel shop job should satisfy the work-hours pathway, but hours must be documented and submitted through his counselor per district rules. Verify the count and the paperwork in August, first week of school at the latest. Then file the Florida Financial Aid Application (FFAA) during senior year, deadline August 31 after graduation, but do it in the fall alongside everything else. Bright Futures is an entitlement program: meet the criteria, get the money. It's the reason his three Florida schools cost $12-15K instead of $19-20K.

**The Benacquisto wildcard.** If Brett is named a National Merit Semifinalist in September 2026 and advances to Finalist, the Benacquisto Scholarship covers full cost of attendance at Florida publics: tuition, fees, room, board, books. That would make UF essentially free. His 1450 PSAT puts him near the Florida cutoff depending on his section breakdown. Watch for the September notification, and if he's named, the Finalist paperwork becomes a top-priority fall task. Verify current Benacquisto funding status with the school when the time comes, as state funding levels have shifted over the years.

**The CSS Profile and your house.** This is the part most homeowner families don't see coming. FAFSA (used by all your publics and Purdue) ignores home equity entirely. The CSS Profile (required by Stanford, MIT, Case, and GT for institutional aid) asks about your ~$300K in home equity and can count some or all of it as an available asset. At $200K income you were already at the thin edge of need eligibility; the home equity pushes your calculated contribution higher at CSS schools. Practical translation: expect little to no need-based aid at Stanford and MIT, and only a modest need-based award at Case. The verified bracket averages I've quoted ($46-50K nets) include families earning barely over $110K; your number will likely be at or above those averages. **Run the NPC at all four CSS schools this summer** so there are no surprises in April.

**FAFSA timing.** The FAFSA for Brett's enrollment year opens October 1, 2026, and will use your 2025 tax return. File it in October even though you won't qualify for need-based grants; it's required for federal loans if you ever want them, for Bright Futures coordination, and some merit programs require it on file. File the CSS Profile in October for the four schools that need it.

**The 529 math.** $60K spread over four years is $15K/year. Layered on your $40K/year cash budget, your true capacity is roughly $40K/year sustainable, or up to ~$50K/year if you're willing to drain the 529 and stretch, with a younger sister four years behind Brett who will need her own plan. Keep her in the frame when you're tempted to stretch for a $50K+ option.

**Merit strategy in one paragraph.** Brett's stats are above the median at every school on this list except MIT and Stanford (where merit doesn't exist anyway). The merit plays, in order of reliability: Alabama (automatic, near-guaranteed, large), Bright Futures (entitlement, guaranteed if hours are filed), Purdue (modest, possible), Case (very likely but insufficient unless he wins Squire or A.W. Smith full tuition), GT Stamps President's Scholars (full COA, lottery odds). Also have Brett search the Palm Beach County community foundation scholarships, his counselor's local list, and databases like Going Merry senior fall; local awards of $500-2,000 stack up.

## Summer Plan (Summer 2026 — the only one left)

This is the last summer before applications, and it's already underway. Priorities in order:

1. **SAT prep with a finish line.** Four to six focused weeks targeting the August test. His PSAT says the ceiling is 1520+. The gap between 1450 and 1510 is worth real money and real odds at GT, MIT, and Case merit. Structured practice tests weekly; his procrastination tendency means a schedule on the wall, not "study when you can."
2. **Essays drafted by late August.** Personal statement (the keyboard business, told through a specific failure-and-fix), MIT short answers, UF honors essay, GT supplements. He writes badly under deadline pressure like every 17-year-old, so the deadline is Labor Day, not November.
3. **Document the business.** Photos, a simple sales/revenue summary, design iterations, customer feedback. This becomes application evidence and scholarship-essay material. Two afternoons of work, huge payoff.
4. **Keep the job, scale the build.** The bagel shop stays (income, maturity signal, Bright Futures hours). If there's spare bandwidth, one ambitious summer build, an F1-inspired project, a CNC upgrade to the keyboard line, whatever he's actually excited about. Genuine, not resume-driven.
5. **Robotics captain prep.** Sketch the season plan and the underclassman training approach now so that when the season starts, the leadership contribution is concrete by the time midyear reports go out.

What NOT to do this summer: do not pay for a late-registration "prestige" summer program (too late to matter, and his business beats them anyway), do not start any new activity, and do not let August arrive with zero essay drafts.

## Probability and Cost Estimates

These are informed estimates based on Brett's profile against verified admission and aid data. They are not outputs of a statistical simulation, and single-digit reaches are genuinely unpredictable at the individual level.

**Headline stats:** Probability of at least one acceptance at or under budget: effectively 100% (FAU, UCF, Alabama are near-certain and all under $26K). Probability of at least one acceptance at an elite engineering program (GT, MIT, Stanford, Purdue-tier): high, driven mostly by Purdue. Probability the April decision involves a genuinely hard money-vs-prestige tradeoff: moderate, and that's a good problem.

**The five April scenarios:**

1. **MIT or Stanford says yes (~9% combined chance).** Cost: $47-50K+, which is $7-10K+/year over budget. The 529 covers the gap. For MIT specifically, given the fit with everything Brett is, I'd say stretch. Family decision, but it's affordable-with-effort, not reckless.
2. **Georgia Tech says yes (~12%).** $44-48K/year, over budget by $4-8K. Elite aero, Southeast, co-op earnings offset some cost. A defensible stretch, closer call than MIT.
3. **Case with standard merit (~most likely Case outcome).** $45-51K/year. Honestly, probably decline unless the Squire or A.W. Smith full-tuition award comes through, in which case Case at ~$20K becomes a top-two option overnight.
4. **Purdue yes, reaches no (a very plausible modal outcome, ~55%).** Top-10 engineering, elite aerospace, $31-34K, under budget. This is a *win*, not a consolation prize.
5. **The floor.** UF at $12-14K, or UCF/FAU at $13-15K, strong engineering, honors college cohort, and roughly $100K+ of your money and 529 preserved for grad school or his sister. Worst case is genuinely good.

## What If the Profile Lands Lower

**If the August SAT comes back ~1400-1440:** Bright Futures FAS is still secure (threshold is 1330), so the Florida schools don't move at all. UF odds dip somewhat but stay live. Purdue stays live. Alabama's automatic merit tier may drop a notch but remains substantial. The reaches get harder: MIT and GT require scores and a 1400 sits below their middle ranges, so those become true moonshots rather than long shots. Strategy change: take the October SAT, and shift emotional energy toward Purdue and UF as the top of the realistic list. Nothing about the financial plan breaks.

**If senior fall grades slip:** The safeties and Alabama's merit are locked on the strength of the first three years. Midyear reports matter at the reaches and at Case merit, so the guardrail is simple: no senior-year course load he can't sustain alongside robotics captaincy and applications. Rigor matters, but a B in AP Physics C beats a collapse.

**If the captain bid or the business plateaus:** The application narrative survives on what already exists: three years of robotics, a real business with real customers, top-tier coursework. The essays carry the story either way. Do not manufacture a last-minute activity; depth already achieved beats breadth added in September.

The reassuring bottom line: this plan's floor is not built on anything that could still go wrong. Bright Futures plus three near-certain Florida admissions plus Alabama means the downside scenario is a good engineering education at $13-26K/year. Everything Brett does between now and November only raises the ceiling.

## What to Do Now

1. **This week:** Register for the August SAT (October backup). Start the prep schedule.
2. **This month:** Run the Net Price Calculators at MIT, Stanford, Case, and Georgia Tech with your real numbers, including home equity. Thirty minutes each, and it eliminates April surprises.
3. **By early August:** Call Boca Raton Community High's counseling office and confirm Brett's Bright Futures service/work hours are documented and sufficient. Get it in writing.
4. **By Labor Day:** Personal statement and MIT essays drafted. Ask the physics teacher and an AP Lang teacher for recommendations in the first week of school.
5. **September:** Submit FAU, UCF, and Alabama applications. Acceptances in hand by Halloween change the whole family's fall.

## Developmental Roadmap

Brett is 16 years old and a rising senior, so this roadmap is short by design. Three years of it already happened, and it went well. What's left is one summer (underway), one senior year, and one final spring. Here's how to run it.

### Where He Stands (Grades 9-11, Complete)

| Area | Status |
|---|---|
| GPA | 3.9 unweighted through junior year. Done. Protect it, don't chase it. |
| Rigor | AP Calc BC, AP Physics 1, AP Lang completed junior year. This is a top-tier load at Boca Raton Community High. |
| Testing | 1450 PSAT. SAT scheduled for Aug 2026. |
| Robotics | 3 years VEX, captain bid pending for senior year. |
| Business | Mechanical keyboard builds with real sales. Ongoing. |
| Athletics | Varsity tennis. Ongoing. |
| Work | Bagel shop, weekends. Ongoing, and doing double duty for Bright Futures hours. |

### Senior Year Course Plan (2026-27)

The goal is simple: the counselor checks "most demanding" on the school report, and Brett doesn't drown while captaining robotics and writing nine applications.

| Slot | Recommended Course | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Math | The most advanced math Boca High offers after Calc BC. If the school offers AP Statistics, multivariable calculus, or dual enrollment calc III through FAU or Palm Beach State, take it. Do not let senior year be a math-free year for a kid applying to MIT. | Continuity of the quantitative thread. Admissions readers at MIT and GT specifically check whether the math sequence continued. |
| Science | AP Physics C (Mechanics, and E&M if offered) | The single most important senior course for this list. If Boca High doesn't offer Physics C, take AP Chemistry or dual enrollment physics and have his counselor note the school's offerings in the report. Rigor is judged relative to what the school makes available, so a missing course won't hurt him if it genuinely isn't offered. |
| English | AP Literature or the strongest available English | Writing is his growth area. A second AP English keeps the humanities half of the transcript credible and preempts the "lopsided STEM kid" read. |
| Social Studies | AP Gov, AP Econ, or AP Psych, whatever fits | Macro or Micro Econ is a nice fit for a kid who runs a business, but any of these works. |
| Elective 1 | Engineering, CAD, computer science, or a technical elective if offered | Feeds the builder narrative and keeps school fun. |
| Elective 2 | Something sustainable | This is the pressure-release valve. One lighter class is fine and smart. A B in AP Physics C beats a collapse across five APs. |

**One honest note on rigor:** four to five APs senior year is the right zone. Six is showing off, and with robotics captaincy plus applications plus the business plus tennis, six is how a 3.9 kid produces a rough midyear report. The midyear report goes to every school that hasn't decided yet, including Stanford, so protect it.

### Senior Year Extracurricular Plan

- **Robotics (Aug-Feb, heavy):** If the captain bid lands, the goal for fall is one nameable, specific leadership contribution. A drivetrain redesign he led, a CAD training program for underclassmen, a scouting system he built. Something he can describe in one concrete sentence on the Common App and in interviews. If the bid doesn't land, three years of contribution still carries the thread. Do not spiral about a title.
- **Keyboard business (steady, don't scale):** Keep it running, document everything (see Maker Portfolio section), but do not take on a huge order backlog in Oct-Nov. Applications come first for eight weeks. The business will be there in Dec.
- **Tennis (spring):** Keep it. It's the balance signal, and by spring the applications are done anyway.
- **Bagel shop (keep until Bright Futures hours are confirmed filed):** Once his counselor confirms the work hours are documented and sufficient, the job becomes optional. My advice: keep it if he likes the money and the routine, drop to fewer shifts during Oct-Nov crunch if needed.
- **Math honor society (maintenance mode):** Attend, participate, done. No need to seek an officer role in a club that isn't his story.

### Summer Plans

**Summer 2026 (now):** Already laid out in the Strategy Brief. SAT prep for the Aug test, essay drafts by Labor Day, business documentation, one ambitious build if he has bandwidth, robotics season planning. That's the whole summer. It's enough.

**Summer 2027 (after graduation, before college):** Plan nothing formal yet, but keep three things in mind. First, if he lands at Purdue, GT, or MIT, engineering summer bridge or early-start programs are worth a look when admitted-student materials arrive in the spring. Second, this is the last summer the keyboard business runs out of your garage, so he may want one final production run or a handoff plan. Third, he'll want to earn some money. College spending cash from a summer of bagels and keyboards is a fine way to close this chapter.

## Activities List (Common App Planning Tool)

Ten slots, in the order they should appear. This is a planning document, not a finished application. [CURRENT] means it's real today and described only as it actually exists. [TARGET] means it's a goal for senior year, and it stays a goal until it happens. Before submitting, every [TARGET] either becomes true or gets rewritten to reflect reality.

**1. Mechanical Keyboard Business, Founder/Builder — [CURRENT]**
Designs, builds, and sells custom mechanical keyboards online. Handles sourcing, assembly, quality control, customer communication, and pricing. This goes first. Before Nov, Brett should pull together his actual numbers (units sold, rough revenue, how long he's been operating) so the description can be specific and true. Goal for the description: one line on what he builds, one line on what running it actually requires.

**2. VEX Robotics Team, 3-Year Member — [CURRENT], Captain — [TARGET]**
Three years on the team is fact. The captaincy is a bid, not a title, until the team or advisor confirms it. If it lands, the description should feature one specific leadership contribution (the training system, the design decision, the season plan), not the word "led" doing all the work. If it doesn't land, three years of hands-on build contribution is still a strong entry.

**3. Part-Time Job, Bagel Shop — [CURRENT]**
Weekend shifts, ongoing. Describe honestly: hours per week, how long he's worked there, what he actually does. Admissions readers consistently rate sustained paid work as a maturity signal, and this one also funds his Bright Futures work-hours requirement. Do not dress it up. "Weekend counter and food prep work, [X] hrs/week since [start date]" is exactly right.

**4. Varsity Tennis — [CURRENT]**
List years on varsity and position honestly. No need to inflate. This entry exists to show he's a whole person who competes and manages a physical commitment alongside everything else.

**5. Math Honor Society, Member — [CURRENT]**
Member, participates in whatever the chapter actually does (tutoring, competitions, meetings). Describe only real involvement. If he does peer tutoring through it, say so; if not, don't.

**6. 3D Printing and Fabrication — [CURRENT]**
This is a genuine sustained hobby that feeds both the business and robotics. Describe it as what it is: self-taught design and printing, iterating parts for keyboards and personal projects. Goal by application time: be able to name two or three specific things he's designed and printed, because specificity is what makes a hobby entry credible.

**7. Maker Portfolio Development — [TARGET]**
Goal: assemble photo and written documentation of keyboard builds and robotics iterations into a portfolio for the MIT application and scholarship submissions (see Maker Portfolio section). This may fold into entries 1 and 2 rather than standing alone; keep the slot flexible.

**8. Robotics Underclassman Training — [TARGET]**
Goal: formalize what he'd do as captain into something nameable, like a CAD onboarding sequence or build-season mentoring for younger members. If this happens in the fall, it becomes real content for entry 2 or its own entry. If it doesn't happen, delete it. Do not write it as done in Oct if it's still a plan.

**9. Formula 1 / Motorsport Engineering Interest — [CURRENT, borderline]**
This only earns a slot if it's more than watching races. If Brett has done real F1-adjacent building (an aero project, a telemetry analysis, a scale model with actual engineering content), it can appear as a hobby entry or feed the "one ambitious summer build" from the summer plan. If it stays a fandom, it lives in an essay or interview answer instead, where genuine enthusiasm plays well without needing to be a resume line.

**10. Community Service Hours — [CURRENT/VERIFY]**
Whatever service hours Brett has accumulated alongside the paid-work pathway. Verify what's actually on file with his counselor in Aug. If the hours are real and meaningful, list them honestly. If the Bright Futures requirement is fully covered by work hours, this slot can go to something real from the list above instead. Do not manufacture a service entry to fill a slot.

**The honest note:** Brett's list is eight real things and two goals. That's a strong position in Jul before senior year. Most applicants pad; he doesn't need to.

## Essay Strategy

### The Common App Personal Statement

The angle was set in the Strategy Brief and it hasn't changed: **the keyboard business, told through a specific failure and fix.** Not a tour of his accomplishments. One moment, examined closely.

The strongest version of this essay picks a single concrete incident. A batch where the stabilizers rattled and a customer said so. A plate whose tolerances were off and the redesign that followed. A pricing mistake that taught him what his time was worth. The reader should come away knowing three things without Brett ever stating them: he thinks like an engineer (hypothesis, test, iterate), he takes responsibility when something he made fails, and he genuinely loves this stuff.

**Structure that works for a writer who isn't a natural writer:** open inside the moment (the customer email, the sound of a bad switch, the misaligned plate on the desk), spend the middle on how he diagnosed and fixed it, and end on what changed in how he builds. Chronological, concrete, no philosophy until the last two paragraphs, and not much even then. Brett's writing is functional, and functional writing about a fascinating specific thing beats ambitious writing about an abstract thing every time.

**Process, because procrastination is the real enemy here:** full ugly draft by Labor Day (already in the plan). Then three revision passes: one with a parent or friend asking "what did you actually do here, show me," one with his AP Lang teacher for prose, one final read-aloud pass for rhythm. Six weeks of intermittent revision on an existing draft is easy. Writing from zero on Oct 25 is the disaster scenario. The draft's existence matters more than its quality.

### Supplemental Essay Map by School

| School | What They Ask (general shape) | Brett's Angle |
|---|---|---|
| MIT | Several short answers: what you do for fun, how you contribute to community, a challenge, why your field | Distribute the threads. Keyboards or F1 for the "fun" prompt (genuine enthusiasm, no polish needed). Robotics teaching for community. A different failure than the personal statement's for the challenge prompt. Mechanical engineering, stated plainly, for the field prompt. MIT's prompts reward directness over eloquence, which suits him. |
| Stanford | Short essays including the roommate letter and what matters to you | The roommate essay is where the keyboard bench, the F1 Sunday routine, and the bagel shop shift schedule become charm. Write it like a text to a friend. The intellectual vitality prompt: the physics question habit his teacher praised, shown through one actual question he chased down. |
| Georgia Tech | Why Georgia Tech, tied to your interests | Specific programs: the co-op, a named lab or research group in mechanical/aerospace he'd actually want, the maker spaces. Generic "great engineering school" essays die at GT. Thirty minutes on the ME department website produces the specifics. |
| Purdue | Short answers on why Purdue and why your major | Same discipline: named facilities, the aero legacy, the hands-on first-year engineering design courses. Connect to the fact that he already builds and sells physical products. |
| Case Western | Why Case, possibly scholarship-specific essays | Two jobs here. The "why Case" essay must name undergrad research access (their claim to fame) and something specific he found. The A.W. Smith Innovation Scholarship materials are where the business gets full treatment: this scholarship targets innovator-builders, and Brett should write it as a founder, with real detail about customers and iteration. |
| UF | Honors essay in the Common App | The honors essay should show intellectual depth beyond stats. The physics-question habit is a good spine: pick one question he asked that led somewhere, and follow it. Required for Honors consideration, and Honors opens Lombardi/Stamps scholarship eligibility, so this essay has money attached. Do not phone it in. |
| FAU (Wilkes Honors) | Honors application materials | Adapt the UF honors essay. Small-cohort honors colleges want evidence he'll show up curious. The business and the teaching thread both work. |
| Alabama, UCF | Minimal or no essays | Whatever short answers exist, adapt existing material. Do not spend fresh energy here. |

### What the Essays Should NOT Be

- **Not a robotics resume in paragraph form.** The activities list already says he did robotics. The essay's job is how he thinks.
- **Not "my love of STEM."** Every engineering applicant writes this. The keyboard failure story is the antidote because it's specific and only Brett can tell it.
- **Not an attempt to sound like a writer he isn't.** Admissions readers smell a thesaurus. Plain, precise, concrete sentences about real objects and real decisions. That's his authentic register and it happens to be exactly how good engineers write.
- **Not a hardship narrative.** He doesn't have one and shouldn't reach for one. A comfortable kid who built something real is a perfectly strong story. Don't manufacture adversity; examine a genuine problem he solved instead.
- **Not about F1 unless it's about him.** "I love Formula 1" is a fandom. "Here's what F1's rapid design iteration taught me about how I run my build process" is an essay. The bar for hobby essays is that the hobby reveals the kid.

## Recommendation Letter Strategy

Brett needs two teacher letters, one counselor letter, and possibly one optional letter. Here's the type of person for each role and how to handle the ask.

### Letter 1: The Physics Teacher (STEM Anchor)

You already know who this is. The teacher who said Brett asks the questions she wishes the whole class would ask is the single most valuable recommender available to him, because that sentence, in her voice, in her letter, is worth more than any award on his list. MIT's readers in particular weight teacher letters heavily and specifically look for evidence of how a student's mind works in a classroom.

**What the letter should convey:** intellectual curiosity in action (the questions), speed of concept acquisition (his known strength), and ideally one specific classroom moment. Teachers write better letters when handed raw material, so Brett should give her a one-page brag sheet: two or three specific class moments he remembers, a short description of the keyboard business and robotics role, and his school list.

**When and how to ask:** first week of school in Aug 2026, in person, not by email. The script is simple: "I'm applying to engineering programs including MIT and Georgia Tech with Nov 1 deadlines. Would you be willing to write me a strong recommendation? I'll give you everything you need well in advance." Teachers get slammed with Oct requests. An Aug ask with materials attached gets a better letter, written with time to spare.

### Letter 2: The Humanities Teacher (The Counterweight)

This should be a teacher who can credibly say the quantitative kid communicates well, which directly preempts the one soft spot in Brett's profile. His AP Lang teacher from junior year is the natural source, since they saw a full year of his analytical writing. The right person is whoever can honestly say some version of: "His writing is clear and his thinking is rigorous, and he improved over the year." A letter that says his prose is precise and his arguments are structured does the job. It doesn't need to claim he's a poet.

**Same timing, same method:** first week of school, in person, brag sheet in hand. If the AP Lang teacher has left the school or the relationship was thin, any humanities or social studies teacher who genuinely knows his work is a better choice than a bigger name who doesn't.

### The Counselor Letter

Boca Raton Community High is a large public school, so his counselor likely knows him thinly. Fix that in Aug. Brett (and honestly, one of you, Josh or Dana) should get fifteen minutes with the counselor early in the fall to make sure three things are on their radar: the keyboard business (counselors rarely know about off-campus ventures), the robotics leadership arc, and the "most demanding curriculum" checkbox given his senior course load. Bring a one-page summary they can pull from. Counselor letters at big publics are often generic by default; a specific one is built by giving the counselor specifics. This is also the same meeting where you confirm the Bright Futures hours paperwork, so it's one trip, two jobs.

### Optional Letter: The Robotics Coach or Employer

Most schools on this list accept one optional recommendation. Use it only if it adds a new dimension. The robotics coach or advisor is the best candidate if the captaincy happens and the coach can speak to how Brett leads, teaches, and handles build-season pressure, which no classroom teacher sees. The bagel shop owner is a dark-horse alternative for Case and the scholarship applications specifically, where reliability and work ethic land well. One optional letter maximum. Three letters saying "great kid, works hard" is worse than two letters saying specific things.

## Maker Portfolio Guidance

Brett is exactly the applicant maker portfolios were invented for, and MIT explicitly accepts one. This is the highest-leverage supplemental material available to him, and most of the work is documentation of things he already does.

### Where It Gets Used

- **MIT:** accepts a maker portfolio as a supplement, reviewed by people with technical backgrounds. This is the primary target.
- **Case Western:** the A.W. Smith Innovation Scholarship application is a natural home for the same material, and Case's interest tracking means engaged, detailed submissions register.
- **GT Stamps President's Scholars and UF Lombardi/Stamps consideration:** if either process invites additional materials or interviews, this portfolio is the show-and-tell.
- **Everywhere else:** even schools with no formal portfolio slot benefit indirectly, because assembling it forces Brett to articulate his process, which sharpens every essay.

### What Goes In It

Aim for three to five documented projects, quality over quantity:

1. **A keyboard build, end to end.** Photos of design or layout planning, sourcing decisions, assembly stages, QC testing, and the finished product. Plus the business layer: how he prices, what customers asked for, what he changed based on feedback. The business dimension is what separates him from hobbyists.
2. **The failure-and-fix story, illustrated.** Whatever incident anchors the personal statement should appear here with photos of the actual problem and the redesign. Essay and portfolio reinforcing each other is a deliberate move.
3. **A robotics contribution he personally owns.** Not the whole team's robot. The subsystem, mechanism, or design iteration that was his, with before/after photos or CAD screenshots and a few sentences on the design tradeoffs.
4. **The summer 2026 ambitious build, if it happens.** An F1-inspired project or a CNC upgrade to the keyboard line would be a great capstone entry.
5. **Optional: a 3D printing project** that shows design iteration (versions of a part, and why each changed).

### How to Document (Start Now, in Jul)

- **Photograph everything from here forward.** Work-in-progress shots, failures included. Failed prints and scrapped plates are portfolio gold because they show iteration. A phone camera and decent light are plenty.
- **For each project, write four short things:** what it is, what problem it solved, what went wrong along the way, what he'd change next time. A few sentences each. Technical reviewers want to see the thinking, not marketing copy.
- **Keep a simple spec sheet for the business:** rough units sold, price range, timeline, tools and processes used. Real numbers, stated plainly. Never inflate; the reviewers build things for a living and inflation reads instantly.
- **Format:** follow MIT's current submission guidelines when the application opens (they specify format and limits through their portal). Build the content now so formatting is a one-evening job in Oct.

**Time budget:** two afternoons in Jul-Aug to backfill photos and write-ups of existing work, then fifteen minutes per week of ongoing documentation during the fall. This is the best effort-to-impact ratio anywhere in Brett's application.

**What a portfolio is not:** a highlight reel of finished, polished objects with no visible process. One beautiful photo of a keyboard says "he has a nice keyboard." Five photos ending in that keyboard, with a paragraph about the tolerance problem in version two, says "admit this kid."
